- Dealing in investments as agent in long-term insurance contracts meeting specified conditions. 

- Advising on and arranging regulated mortgage contracts. 

- Advising on and arranging regulated home revision and home purchase plans. 

- Assisting in the administration and performance of a contract of insurance. 

- Providing basic advice on stakeholder products. 

In particular, appointed representatives are not permitted to deal in investments either as agent or principal, or manage investments. 

Following the publication in 2021 of its consultation, the FCA published its Policy Statement (PS22/11) in 2022, with the requirements coming into effect in December 2022. 

The aim of the FCA’s consultation was to reduce potential harm to consumers and markets. The FCA has been clear that its proposals used results of their thematic reviews undertaken on the general insurance sector in 2016 and the investment management sector in 2019. 

While the FCA has broad powers in relation to setting the requirements that apply to principals, more fundamental reforms of the AR regime would require the government to propose amendments to the legislation – hence the purpose of HM’s Treasury call for evidence. 

HM Treasury noted that if reform was required, four key elements to the regulatory approach which need to be changed to the legislation to be enacted: 

- the overall scope of the Section 39 exemption, including the regulated activities which ARs are permitted to carry on 

- the regulatory tools available to the FCA, should it be concluded that the FCA should be empowered to do more to prevent abuse of the AR regime 

- whether more direct regulatory requirements should be placed on ARs in order to strengthen incentives for regulatory compliance and high standards of conduct, and 

- the role of the **Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS)** in relation to ARs and their principals where consumers have experienced detriment while dealing with an AR. 

The following changes came into effect: 

- Additional information on ARs and notification requirements for principals: 

   - This will allow the FCA to easily identify potential risks within principals and ARs. 

   - It will also help it to assess whether the principal has the expertise, systems and controls to effectively oversee its ARs. 

   - the FCA will be able to target its supervisory interventions more effectively. 

- Clarifying and strengthening the responsibilities and expectations of principals: 

   - Providing additional guidance for principals on their responsibilities. 

   - Providing FCA expectations on how they should act and oversee their ARs. 

38 

Regulatory Framework 

## Recognised Investment Exchanges (RIEs) and Recognised Clearing Houses (RCHs) 

Trading in, and issuance of, securities is conducted through formal exchanges; for example, the **London Stock Exchange (LSE)** . There are often separate clearing systems connected to these formal exchanges which facilitate the settlement of the trades which take place. The firms operating these systems are referred to as clearing houses. 

FSMA gives the FCA the responsibility of recognising, regulating and supervising these exchanges and clearing houses. 

Once recognised, these exchanges are referred to as RIEs, and clearing houses are referred to as RCHs. RIEs and RCHs are classified as exempt persons in that they do not need to seek authorisation from the FCA in order to carry on their regulated activities; they are, instead, recognised. 

## Miscellaneous Exempt Persons 

HM Treasury has established certain exemptions from which certain persons do not need to be authorised. Some of these exemptions are restricted in that they only apply under certain circumstances. For example, supranational bodies of which the UK or another **European Economic Area (EEA)** member state is a member, and both the UK central bank or a central bank of an EEA member state, are exempted from the need to be authorised to carry on any regulated activity, apart from the need to effect, or carry out contracts of insurance. 

In contrast, certain bodies are exempted from the need to be authorised for the sole regulated activity of accepting deposits; these include municipal banks, local authorities and charities. As stated in FSMA 2000 (Exemption) Order 2001, the main organisations that are exempt in respect of any regulated activity other than for insurance business, are: 

- the **Bank of England (BoE)** 

- the central banks of EEA member states 

- the European Central Bank (ECB) 

- the European Investment Bank (EIB) 

- the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) 

- the International Monetary Fund (IMF), and 

- the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD). 

## Exemption from the General Prohibition 

Certain persons are able to perform limited regulated activities without the need to be authorised or exempt. In effect, the general prohibition section within FSMA does not apply to them. Such persons include the members of Lloyd’s insurance market and certain DPBs. 

## Members of Lloyd’s 

Several activities carried on in connection with business at Lloyd’s are regulated activities. These include: 

- advising on syndicate participation 

- acting as a managing agent for one or more syndicates, and 

- arranging deals in insurance contracts. 

39 

However, FSMA disapplies the general prohibition for members of Lloyd’s in relation to contracts of insurance written at Lloyd’s. 

The reason why the general prohibition does not apply here is because the FCA expects the activities at Lloyd’s to be suitably supervised and executed by the Society of Lloyd’s, and therefore additional FCA authorisation of its members is unnecessary. The FCA does, however, have certain powers to impose additional rules on the members (or former members) of Lloyd’s if it is felt necessary. 

## Members of the Professions 

There are five professions where individual firms are permitted to carry on particular regulated activities without the need to apply to the FCA for regulated permission. Professional firms are required to apply to their relevant professional body for permission to conduct these regulated activities. The individual professions include: 

- accountants 

- solicitors 

- actuaries 

- chartered surveyors, and 

- licensed conveyancers. 

Designated Professional Bodies (DPBs) are the professional bodies which are able to grant permissions. They include the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales (ICAEW), the Law Society, the Institute and Faculty of Actuaries and the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (RICS). 

The DPB must operate a set of rules with which its members must comply, and the regulated activity must be incidental to the provision of its professional services. For example, a firm of accountants providing tax advice might give a client advice in relation to which investments might best be sold to avoid the accrual of a tax liability. There are other restrictions to the activities carried out by DPBs member firms. For example, such firms are allowed to receive a financial reward only from the client (ie, they should not receive commissions from the providers of products held or bought by the client); and certain regulated activities may not be carried out by the members, namely: 

- accepting deposits 

- dealing in investments as principal 

- establishing, operating or winding up CISs or stakeholder pension schemes 

- effecting or carrying out contracts of insurance (including Lloyd’s business) 

- providing funeral plan contracts 

- issuing electronic money (e-money) 

- providing basic advice on stakeholder products, and 

- establishing a pension scheme. 

40 

Regulatory Framework 

## 1.4 Permission to Carry on Regulated Activities (Part IV) 

In granting the permission to carry on a regulated activity, the regulator must be satisfied that the applicant will satisfy, and continue to satisfy, the threshold conditions in relation to all of the regulated activities for which they will have permission. See chapter 2, section 8. 

The regulator may incorporate in the description of the regulated activity such limitations (as to the circumstances in which the activity may, or may not, be carried on) as it considers appropriate, or specify a narrower or wider description of regulated activity than that to which the application relates. It could also give permission for the carrying on of a regulated activity which is not included among those to which the application relates. 

The regulator may, on receipt of an application of an authorised person, vary the permission by: 

- adding a regulated activity for which it gives permission 

- removing a regulated activity from those for which it gives permission 

- cancelling a requirement imposed under Section 43 (Imposition of Requirements),or 

- varying such a requirement. 

The regulator may exercise its powers in relation to an authorised person if it appears that: 

- they are failing, or are likely to fail, to satisfy the threshold conditions, or 

- they have failed, during a period of at least 12 months, to carry on a regulated activity for which they have a permission. 

When control of an authorised person changes hands, if it believes that the effect of the acquisition is uncertain, the regulator may vary the authorised person’s permission by imposing a requirement or varying a requirement included in the authorised person’s permission. 

## 1.5 Performance of Regulated Activities (Part V) 

The regulators (ie, the PRA and/or FCA) may make an order (prohibition order) prohibiting an individual from performing a specified function, any function falling within a specified description, or simply any function. 

A prohibition order may relate to a specified regulated activity, any regulated activity falling within a specified description or all regulated activities, to an authorised person generally. 

The regulators must provide the individual concerned with a warning notice, informing them that they propose to make a prohibition order. If the regulators decide to make a prohibition order, they must give the individual concerned a decision notice. The individual concerned may refer the matter to the Upper Tribunal, which deals with appeals by firms and/or individuals against FCA decision notices or supervisory notices. 

Authorised persons must take reasonable care to ensure that a person does not perform either a Senior Management Function (defined under the SM&CR – refer to chapter 2, section 2) or a Controlled Function (defined under the Approved Persons Regime (APER), refer to chapter 2, section 3), under an arrangement entered into by the authorised person (including a contractor of the authorised person) in 

41 

relation to the carrying on by the authorised person of a regulated activity, unless approved by the PRA and/or the FCA. 

The regulators may impose a penalty if they are satisfied that a person has at any time performed the activity requiring approval where at the time the person knew, or could reasonably be expected to have known, that they were performing an activity without the required approval. 

The regulators are very likely to take action against a person if it appears that they are guilty of misconduct and if the regulators are satisfied that it is appropriate in all circumstances to take action against the person involved. 

## 1.6 Disciplinary Measures (Part XIV) 

If the regulators consider that an authorised person has contravened a requirement imposed on them under FSMA or UK law (ie, EU regulation onshored under the European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act), then they may publish a statement to that effect. 

In addition, FSMA also gives the regulators the power to impose a financial penalty in respect of a contravention, of whatever amount is appropriate. 

FSMA also gives the regulators the power to suspend or impose limitations on the authorised person, for a period of time, when regulators consider that the individual has contravened a requirement imposed on them. 

FSMA dictates that the authorities must provide a warning notice to the authorised person if the regulators are going to take, or are proposing to take, disciplinary action. 

## 1.7 General Rule-Making Power (Part X) 

FSMA provides the regulators (the PRA and the FCA) with general rule-making powers that apply to authorised persons carrying out all activities whether regulated or not. 

In this respect, the regulators have the power to make rules regarding: 

- the handling of client money 

- the requirement for firms to have a remuneration policy 

- recovery and resolution plans for banking institutions 

- insurance business rules 

- price-stabilising rules, including the manner in which they are permitted, the conditions, circumstances and times when, and/or the period during which they are permitted 

- financial promotion rules 

- **money laundering (ML)** rules 

- control of information rules 

- modification of waiver 

- contravention of rules, including the evidential provisions, action for damages and the limits on effect of contravening rules, and 

- procedural provisions, including the notification of rules to the Treasury, rule-making instruments, verification of rules and consultation and general supplementary powers. 

42 

Regulatory Framework 

## 1.8 Injunctions and Restitution (Part XXV) and Penalties for Market Abuse (Part VIII) 

FSMA empowers the FCA to be able to take action against individuals, and/or firms for carrying out or conducting market abuse. Under these circumstances, the FCA is responsible for applying to the courts to seek an injunction against any individual, and/or firm conducting such market abuse. 

FSMA provides the FCA with the power to impose penalties for market abuse, which also includes **inside information** and **insider dealing** . 

Refer to chapter 3, section 3 for more details on the UK’s market abuse regime and rules. 

## 1.9 Offences (Part XXVII) 

FSMA provides the FCA with powers in relation to how the regulator should deal with individuals, and/ or authorised persons who have made **misleading statements** or undertaken unacceptable practices. It also gives the regulator the power to instigate proceedings for an offence under: Part V of the **Criminal Justice Act (CJA) 1993** (insider dealing); prescribed regulations relating to ML; and Schedule 7 of the Counter-Terrorism Act 2008 (terrorist financing or money laundering). 

Refer to chapter 3, section 4 for more details on the UK’s money laundering regulation and rules. 

## 1.10 Collective Investment Schemes (CISs) (Part XVII) 

FSMA defines what is meant by the arrangements for and identifies the restrictions in place in relation to the promotion of CISs. 

FSMA also provides powers in order to review, approve, and refuse applications on authorised unit trust (AUT) schemes. 

## 1.11 Additional Powers Granted under the Financial Services Act 2010 

The Financial Services Act 2010, effective from 8 June 2010, gave additional powers to the then regulator, the FSA, (which also included amendments to FSMA). The additional powers were: 

- the power to impose suspensions or restrictions on authorised persons, under s206A FSMA, and on **approved persons** , under s66 FSMA (the suspension power), and 

- the power to impose penalties on persons who perform controlled functions without approval, under s63A FSMA (the non-approved person’s penalty power). 

## 1.11.1 The Power to Suspend 

If any authorised person breaches any regulatory rules or requirements, the power to suspend enables the regulator to suspend any permissions the person may have to carry on a regulated activity, or impose restrictions on that person carrying on a regulated activity. 

43 

The suspension power enables the regulator to suspend an approved person from performing one or more controlled functions for which they are approved, or restrict the performance of the controlled function by them. 

In addition, the regulators can impose a suspension on an authorised person for a period of up to 12 months and on an approved person for a period of up to two years. 

The suspension power is an alternative sanction to a financial penalty which could be imposed by the regulator. 

The suspension power has been seen as a major change of emphasis and focus by industry, including the way in which the regulator can operate. To alleviate industry concerns, the regulator states that it would only use this power when it considers that the imposition of a suspension would be the most effective and persuasive deterrent, rather than the imposition of a financial penalty alone. 

The **Regulatory Decisions Committee (RDC)** is responsible for providing warning and decision notices when the suspension power is used. 

## 1.11.2 The Non-Approved Persons Penalty Power 

The non-approved persons penalty power provides the regulator with the ability to impose a financial penalty on a person, of an amount the regulator considers appropriate, when it is satisfied that: 

- the person has at any time performed a controlled function without approval, and 

- at that time the person knew, or could reasonably be expected to have known, that they were performing a controlled function without approval. 

## 1.12 Financial Promotions Order (FPO) 

A financial promotion is defined in Section 21 of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (FSMA) as: 

_‘an invitation or inducement to engage in investment activity, communicated by a person in the course of business’._ 

The effect of section 21 of the Act (Restrictions on financial promotion) is that in the course of business, an unauthorised person must not communicate an invitation or inducement to engage in investment activity or to engage in claims management activity unless either the content of the communication is approved for the purposes of section 21 by an authorised person or it is exempt. 

The FPO is made up of the following parts: 

- **Part I** – Citation, Commencement and interpretation 

- **Part II** – Controlled Activities and Controlled Investments 

- **Part III** – Exemptions: Interpretation and Application 

- **Part IV** – Exempt communications: All controlled Activities 

- **Part V** – Exempt Communications: Deposits and Insurance 

- **Part VI** – Exempt Communications: Certain Controlled Activities 

- **Part VIA** – Exempt Communications: Controlled Claims Management Activities. 

44 

Regulatory Framework 

In the UK, it is a criminal offence to communicate a financial promotion unless it has been made or approved by a firm authorised under the UK Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (FSMA) or it qualifies for an exemption. 

An investment activity is defined as a controlled activity that is performed in respect of a controlled investment. This means that the regime not only covers promotions for the straightforward sale of a controlled investment, but also promotions as it pertains to investments. 

Controlled investments include shares, bonds and derivatives, and Controlled activities include dealing, providing portfolio management or providing investment advice. 

The UK regime covers all financial promotions that are capable of having an effect in the UK, including adverts from abroad that are targeted at UK investors. 

## 1.13 Bank of England (BoE) Act 1988 

The Bank of England (BoE) Act makes provisions in relation to the constitution, regulation, financial arrangements and functions of the BoE. This also includes the provision for the transfer of supervisory functions and to amend the Banking Act 1987. 

The Act provides an objective in relation to the Bank to protect and enhance the stability of the financial systems of the UK. 

In doing so, the Bank created an oversight committee, the functions of which are to keep under review the Bank’s performance in relation to: 

- the Bank’s objective 

- monitoring the extent to which the Bank’s financial management has been met in relation to the court’s set objectives for the directors of the Bank, and 

- keeping the internal controls of the Bank under review, with a view to securing the proper conduct of its financial affairs. 

The Bank must provide HM Treasury with a copy of reports prepared by the oversight committee. 

The Bank must also make available to the Chancellor of the Exchequer a report of its activities. 

The court of directors determines the Bank’s strategy in relation to the financial stability objective, and from time to time reviews this strategy. Before determining or revising the Bank’s financial strategy, the court of directors must consult on the revised strategy with the **Financial Policy Committee (FPC)** and HM Treasury. 

The Bank set up an ‘FPC’ as a subcommittee of the Bank. 

45 

## 1.14 Banking Act 2009 

The Banking Act 2009 created a Special Resolution Regime (SRR) which gave the UK regulators a permanent framework, providing the regulatory resolution tools for dealing with UK banks and building societies at serious risk of failure. The SRR powers came into force in 2009. 

The Act sets out seven key objectives which must be considered when choosing which resolution tools to use to: 

- protect and enhance the stability of the UK financial system 

- protect and enhance public confidence in the stability of the banking systems of the UK 

- protect depositors and investors covered by relevant compensation schemes 

- protect, where relevant, **client assets** 

- protect public funds, and minimise reliance on public financial support 

- avoid interfering with property rights in contravention of the Human Rights Act 1998, and 

- ensure the continuity of banking services and critical functions in the UK. 

The SRR powers allows the regulatory authorities to: 

- transfer all or part of a bank’s business (its shares or property, ie, assets and liabilities) to a private sector purchaser 

- transfer all or part of a bank’s property to a bridge bank – a subsidiary of the BoE – pending a future sale 

- place a bank into temporary public ownership (this is a Treasury decision) 

- apply to place a bank into the bank insolvency procedure (BIP), which is designed to allow for rapid payments to the **Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS)** , insured depositors (or transfer of their accounts to a healthy bank), and/or 

- apply to use the bank administration procedure (BAP) to deal with a part of a bank which has not transferred and has been put into administration. 

The Banking Act creates clearly defined roles for the operation of the SRR. The PRA, in consultation with the Bank and Treasury, will make the ultimate decision whether to place a bank into the SRR. The Treasury decides whether to place a bank into temporary public ownership. 

## 1.15 Financial Services Act 2012 – Twin Peaks Regulatory Structure 

## The Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) 

The Bank of England and Financial Services (BEFSA) Act 2016 (the Act) ended the status of the PRA as a subsidiary of the BoE by making the BoE the PRA. The Act also created the Prudential Regulation Committee (PRC), through which the BoE exercises its functions as the PRA. 

The PRA is a focused prudential regulator, with responsibility for the prudential supervision of banks, building societies, insurers, friendly societies, credit unions, Lloyd’s of London and its managing agents, and certain significant investment firms. 

46 

Regulatory Framework 

The PRA’s role is to contribute to the promotion of the stability of the UK financial system through the microprudential regulation of those firms within its regulatory remit. It has an overall objective to promote the safety and soundness of regulated firms and will meet this objective primarily by seeking to minimise any adverse effects of firm failure on the UK financial system, by ensuring that firms carry on their business in a way that avoids adverse effects on the UK financial system. 

In relation to insurance supervision, the PRA has two complementary objectives: 

- Firstly, to **secure an appropriate degree of protection for policyholders** . 

- Secondly, and as needed, to **minimise the adverse impact** that the failure of an insurer, or the way in which an insurer carries out its business, could have on the stability of the UK financial system. 

The PRA will achieve this by: 

- aiming to avoid failures which are a cost to the economy 

- emphasising resolution planning in order to permit orderly failure 

- cooperating closely with the FPC and the FCA to ensure macro and microprudential regulation is aligned across all markets, and 

- working with the FCA and others to ensure the UK authorities have a strong voice in international policy-making. 

## The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) 

The FCA’s role is to ensure that the relevant markets for financial services in the UK function well. It will achieve this strategic objective by: 

- securing an appropriate degree of protection for consumers 

- protecting and enhancing the integrity of the UK financial system, and 

- promoting effective competition in the interests of consumers. 

It takes a proportionate approach to regulation, adapting to the needs of different consumers and market participants, and sets and enforces clear expectations for firms and market participants. 

The FCA has a duty to promote competition unless this is incompatible with its strategic and operational objectives. It also has a separate duty to minimise the extent to which regulated businesses may be used for purposes connected with financial crime. 

In addition to its stated objective, the FCA: 

- focuses on the conduct regulation of all firms, covering their dealings with retail customers through to their activities in the wholesale markets. The FCA regulates over 50,000 firms, including those prudentially supervised by the PRA 

- is responsible for the prudential supervision of all firms not prudentially supervised by the PRA 

- supervises trading infrastructure, including the investment exchanges and over-the-counter (OTC) markets, and monitors firms’ compliance with the UK Market Abuse regime 

- has criminal powers to investigate and prosecute insider dealing 

- acts as the UK Listing Authority (UKLA), and 

- is responsible for overseeing the **Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS)** and jointly responsible with the PRA for the FSCS. 

47 

Following the **Financial Services and Markets Act 2023 (FSMA 2023)** being published, and the publication of the FSMA (Commencement No.1) Regulations – from 29 August 2023, the FCA (along with the PRA) have a new secondary objective to facilitate the international competitiveness of the UK economy (in particular, the financial services sector) and its medium-to long-term growth, subject to aligning with relevant international standards. 

## 1.15.1 The Financial Services Act 2012 

The Act is divided into two volumes. Volume I contains the clauses and Volume II contains the Schedules to the Act. The Act replaces and amends parts of FSMA 2000. 

Below is a list of the main changes contained in Volume I of the Act which impact FSMA-regulated firms. _Note: large parts of the Act refer simply to the ‘authority’, which meant at the time the ‘Financial Services Authority (FSA); following the restructure of financial services in the UK in 2013, the reference to ‘authority’ now means either the FCA or the PRA._ 

## Volume I 

## Part 1 – Bank of England (BoE) 

- **Deputy governors** 

   - The Act specifies that, in addition to the Governor of the BoE, there should also be a deputy governor for financial stability, monetary policy and prudential regulation. In addition, there should be no more than nine non-executive directors (NEDs). 

- **Bank’s financial stability objective** 

   - The Act amends the stability objective for the BoE. Instead of ‘ _contribute to protecting and enhancing_ ’ financial stability, the Bank is now tasked to ‘ _protect and enhance_ ’ the financial stability in the UK. 

- **Financial stability strategy and Financial Policy Committee (FPC)** 

   - The BoE created a subcommittee (the Oversight Committee) consisting of the NEDs, with the purpose of keeping under review the Bank’s performance in relation to its objectives, the duty of the FPC and the Bank’s strategy. In addition, the Committee will monitor the extent to which the objectives have been met, keeping under review the internal financial controls of the Bank, with a view to securing the proper conduct of the Bank’s financial affairs. 

## Part 2 – Amendments of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (FSMA) 

- **The regulators (FCA/PRA)** 

   - The FCA’s strategic objective is ensuring that the relevant markets function well. In addition, it also has a number of operational objectives which relate to consumer protection, integrity and competition. 

   - In terms of its consumer protection, this means securing an ‘ _appropriate degree of protection for consumers_ ’. 

   - In terms of integrity, this refers to protecting and enhancing the integrity of the UK financial system. 

   - The competition objective promotes effective competition in the interests of consumers in the markets for regulated financial services, and services provided by RIEs. The FCA must maintain arrangements to supervise, monitor and be able to take appropriate enforcement action against authorised persons. It must consult practitioners and consumers on the extent to which its general policies and practices are consistent with its general duties. In satisfying these 

48 

Regulatory Framework 

requirements, the FCA must also make arrangements to establish a practitioner panel, a smaller business practitioner panel, a markets practitioner panel and a consumer panel. 

   - The PRA’s general objective is promoting the safety and soundness of PRA-authorised persons. The PRA will seek to satisfy this objective by looking to ensure that the business of PRAauthorised persons is carried on in a way which avoids any adverse effect on the stability of the UK financial system and seeks to minimise the adverse effect that the failure of a PRA-authorised person could be expected to have on the stability of the UK financial system. 

   - The PRA also has an insurance objective, which is to contribute to the securing of an appropriate degree of protection for those who are or who may become policyholders. The PRA must make arrangements to establish a practitioner panel. 

- **Extension of scope of regulation** 

The Act added two new regulated activities: 

1. Where a regulated activity is carried on by way of business. 

2. Relating to information about a person’s financial standing and the setting of financial benchmarks. 

This draws into regulated business the need for a person to be an approved person, and includes anyone who contributes to the setting of financial benchmarks. This change occurred because of the LIBOR (London Interbank Offered Rate) rate-setting issue. 

- **Designation of activities requiring prudential regulation by the PRA** 

   - HM Treasury can specify which regulated activities will be supervised by the PRA, and therefore become PRA-regulated activities, although the PRA can also provide for appropriate exceptions to be applied. 

- **Permission to carry on regulated activities** 

   - The Act highlights that regulatory permission to carry on one or more permitted regulated activities must be made to the appropriate regulator; for example, the PRA where the regulated activities consist of PRA-regulated activities or the applicant (firm) is a PRA-authorised person, and to the FCA in any other case. This could mean that an authorised firm could be dually authorised by the PRA and the FCA depending on the type of firm and the regulated activities which it undertakes. Other firms would be authorised by either the PRA or the FCA. 

- **Prohibition orders** FSMA Section 56 is amended so that both the FCA and the PRA are able to make a prohibition order if the regulators can evidence that an individual is not a **fit and proper** person to perform their functions in relation to a regulated activity. 

- **Rules and guidance (general rule-making powers of the FCA and PRA)** The Act provides the FCA and the PRA with powers allowing them to make rules applying to authorised persons, with respect to the carrying on by them of regulated activities or the carrying on by them of activities which are not regulated activities. 

   - The FCA also has powers to set rules requiring authorised persons to take specified steps in connection with the setting of a financial benchmark by a specified person. 

- **Powers in relation to RIEs and clearing houses** The Act identifies that the FCA is the appropriate regulator for RIEs, and the BoE is the appropriate regulator for RCHs. 

- **Power to take disciplinary measures against recognised bodies** 

   - The Act provides public censure powers to both the regulators to publish a statement if they consider that a recognised body has contravened a relevant requirement imposed on that body. Both regulators can impose a financial penalty upon that body, an amount of which the regulators may consider appropriate. 

49 

- **Discipline and enforcement** 

The Act provides that, where HM Treasury considers it necessary, no public disclosure will be made in respect of a warning notice made by the regulators, in addition to the fact that the person receiving the warning notice must not publish any details concerning the notice issued. 

- **Provisions about consumer protection and competition** The Act provides for a designated consumer body to make a complaint to the FCA if it believes a feature, or a combination of features appear to be significantly damaging the interest of consumers and/or the market for financial services. Consumer bodies are designated by HM Treasury. 

## Part 4 – Collaboration between the HM Treasury and the BoE, FCA or PRA 

- **Duty of Bank to notify the HM Treasury of the possible need for public funds** When it appears to the BoE that there is a material risk of the following happening, then it must notify HM Treasury: 

   - when it is expected to provide financial assistance to or in respect of a financial institution 

   - when there is an expectation that there will be an exercise of the respective powers provided under Parts 1 to 3 of the Banking Act 2009 and the costs associated with the exercise of these powers, and 

   - when the manager of the FSCS might reasonably request a loan from the National Loans Fund or financial assistance from HM Treasury. 

- **Duty of the HM Treasury, Bank and PRA to coordinate discharge of function** 

   - The Act specifies that HM Treasury and the BoE (including the PRA) must arrange to coordinate the discharge of their respective functions so far as they relate to the stability of the UK financial system and affect public interest. 

- **Memorandum of understanding (MoU): crisis management** HM Treasury, the BoE and the PRA must prepare and maintain a memorandum describing in general terms how they intend to comply with their duty to coordinate their supervisory activities. HM Treasury must lay before parliament a copy of this memorandum. 

- **MoU: international organisations** 

   - The Act stipulates that HM Treasury, the BoE and the FCA/PRA must prepare and maintain a memorandum describing how they intend to coordinate the exercise of their relevant functions so far as they relate to their engagement on and with international organisations. 

## Part 5 – Inquiries and Investigations 

- **Cases in which the HM Treasury may arrange independent inquiries** 

   - HM Treasury may arrange separate inquiries which may relate to events occurring in respect of CISs, a person carrying on a regulated activity, listed securities or an issuer of listed securities, particularly when the events may pose a threat to the stability of the UK financial system. In addition, HM Treasury may arrange an inquiry in relation to an event which occurs in respect of an RCH, or a recognised interbank payment system, which poses or could pose a serious threat to the financial stability of, or confidence in, the UK financial system. 

- **Power to appoint a person to hold an inquiry** 

   - The Act provides the framework for HM Treasury in respect of the scope of any inquiry, the period, conduct, and the direction of the inquiry, including the making of any reports. 

• **Powers of appointed person and procedure** The Act details the powers the person has who is appointed to hold an inquiry and the procedures to be followed, including obtaining such information as is required. 

50 

Regulatory Framework 

- **Duty of the FCA and the PRA to investigate and report on possible regulatory failure** The Act provides for instances where the regulators must undertake an investigation when an event has occurred in relation to a regulated person or a CIS which indicates a significant failure to secure an appropriate degree of protection for consumers, or which could have a significant effect on the integrity of the UK financial system. 

- **Conduct of investigation** 

The Act states that it is for the regulators to decide how best such investigations are to be carried out. HM Treasury may, however, direct the regulators in relation to the control of the scope and period over which the investigation is to be carried out, the conduct of the investigation and the making of the report. 

- **Statements of policy** 

The Act requires both regulators to prepare and issue a statement of their policy intentions in relation to the exercise of their functions under the duty to conduct an investigation. 

## Part 6 – Investigation of Complaints against Regulators 

- **Arrangements for the investigation of complaints** 

The Act requires both the FCA, and/or the PRA to make the necessary arrangements for the investigation of complaints arising in connection with the exercise of, or failure to exercise, any of their relevant regulatory functions, and in doing so, to appoint an independent person to be responsible and accountable for the conduct of investigations in accordance with the complaint’s regulators scheme. 

- **Investigation of complaints** 

The Act provides that the regulators are not necessarily obliged to investigate a complaint, in accordance with the complaints scheme, particularly if the regulators reasonably consider it would be more appropriate for the complaint to be dealt with in another way (ie, referring the complaint to the Upper Tribunal or by the issuing of other legal proceedings). 

## 1.16 Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 

The **Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013** implemented some of the recommendations provided by the Independent Commission on Banking, chaired by Sir John Vickers, in relation to the ring-fencing requirements for the banking sector. Other reforms include the closer regulation of banking executives and the creation of individual accountability by establishing the **Senior Managers & Certification Regime (SM&CR)** , and the creation of a new criminal offence in relation to mismanagement leading to the failure of a bank. The Act also introduced a bail-in **stabilisation** option which forms part of the special resolution regime (SRR), the introduction of a payment systems regulator, and further powers which allow the FCA to take measures to limit the special resolution regime, and further allow for the introduction of the payment systems regulator. 

It also implemented the key recommendations of the Parliamentary Commission on Banking Standards (PCBS), which tackled and provided an urgent review of the professional standards and culture in the banking industry following the revelations of the LIBOR rate manipulation. 

The Banking Reform Act is made up of eight parts. These are as follows: 

- **Part 1** – Ring-fencing. 

- **Part 2** – Depositor and the FSCS. 

- **Part 3** – Bail-in stabilisation option. 

51 

- **Part 4** – Conduct of persons working in financial services sector. 

- **Part 5** – Regulation of payment systems. 

- **Part 6** – Special administration for operators of certain infrastructure systems. 

- **Part 7** – Miscellaneous. 

- **Part 8** – Final provisions. 

The most relevant parts of the Act are as follows. 

## Part 1 – Ring-Fencing 

The underlying rationale of the ring-fencing regime is to insulate critical banking services from shocks to the financial system. The ring-fencing regime separates core retail banking services, which are of most importance to private individuals as well as smaller companies, from the investment banking services. 

Core activities presently consist of accepting deposits or other payments, facilities for withdrawing money and for overdrafts. A UK-incorporated institution, which carries on these activities (a ring-fenced bank) will be subject to the regime unless it is regarded as a type of entity which is exempted; for example, certain categories of deposit-taking institutions, including building societies, credit unions, or banks which hold deposits below £25 billion. UK branches of non-UK banks will not be subject to the ring-fencing regime requirements, although UK-incorporated bank subsidiaries of non-UK banking groups are within its scope. These provisions came into force in 2019. 

HM Treasury proposed a review of the ‘ring-fencing’ requirements as part of its Edinburgh Reform Regime – refer to chapter 5, section 1.3 for more details. 

## Part 4 – Conduct of a Person Working in the Financial Services Sector 

This is known as ‘SM&CR’, which initially applied to banks, UK branches of foreign banks, and insurance companies, but has been extended to apply to most regulated firms. 

This is discussed in greater detail in chapter 2, section 2. 

## 1.17 Bank of England and Financial Services Act (BEFSA) 2016 

The Act is the third major piece of legislation that the government made to fundamentally reform the financial sector. 

Of relevance is Sections 21 to 26, as they relate to the conduct of a person working in the financial services sector: 

- **Section 21** – extending to authorised persons provisions of Part 5 of the FSMA 2000 (performance of regulated activities) which applies only to relevant authorised persons. 

- **Section 22** – provides powers to the FCA and the PRA to make transitional provisions. 

- **Section 23** – inserts the PRA and the FCA as the administrators of the **Senior Managers Regime** . 

- **Section 24** – rules of conduct (minor cosmetic changes). 

- **Section 25** – misconduct (minor cosmetic changes). 

- **Section 26** – decisions causing a financial institution to fail: meaning of insolvency. 

52 

Regulatory Framework 

## 1.18 UK Financial Services Act 2021 

The 2019 Queen’s Speech background paper expressed a desire to bring forward, in the upcoming parliamentary session, legislation to: 

_‘ensure that the UK maintains its world-leading regulatory standards and remains open to international markets after we leave the EU’_ . 

When introduced to the House of Commons, the Bill’s stated objectives were to: enhance the UK’s world-leading prudential standards and promote financial stability, promote openness between the UK and international markets, and maintain an effective financial services regulatory framework and sound capital markets. However, HM Treasury added a fourth objective in recognition of amendments made during the Bill’s passage through Parliament, which is to: _‘protect consumers who use a range of financial services’_ . 

In April 2021, the **Financial Services Act 2021** received Royal Assent and came into effect. 

It was the UK’s first step, post-Brexit, in re-establishing control of financial services regulations and ensuring that the UK remains an ‘open and dynamic financial centre’. 

Although most of the Act seeks to shape the regulatory framework for UK financial services, it also contains a range of measures unconnected to Brexit. These included the following: 

- Part 4A Permissions 

- Duty of care 

- Refer to chapter 2, section 6 for further details on the FCA’s ‘ **Consumer Duty** ’ initiative and rules. 

- • Money laundering 

   - Offences, forfeiture of money and overseas trustees 

- Debt respite scheme 

- Help to save 

- Buy now, pay later (BNPL) products 

- Financial collateral 

- Payment services and the provision of cash. 

The post-Brexit reforms included the following: 

- UK Prudential framework 

   - Investment firm’s Prudential regime, ie, the FCA’s Investment Firms Prudential requirements for MiFID firms 

   - Basel III and 3.1 standards 

- Access to financial markets 

   - Overseas funds regime 

   - UK MiFIR equivalence 

   - PRIIPS 

   - UK EMIR 

- Insider dealing and market abuse 

   - Maintaining insider lists 

   - Transactions by senior managers 

   - Personal data 

53 

- ‘IBOR’ 

   - Providing the FCA with the necessary powers to manage the cessation of critical ‘LIBOR’ benchmarks in a manner to protect consumers and ensures market integrity. 

Further details on ‘insider dealing and market abuse’ can be found in chapter 3, section 3. 

With regards to ‘UK Prudential Framework’, the following is a summary of the Investment Firms Prudential Regime (IFPR). 

The UK IFPR follows the EU’s Investment Firms Directive and Regulations, which came into effect in June 2021, given the UK was heavily involved with the development of the legislation. 

However, following Brexit, the UK undertook some minor amendments to ensure that it is fit for purpose for the UK market. 

The UK Regime came into effect on 1 January 2022. 

The approach under the IFPR is to consider the harm an FCA investment firm can cause to others based on the activities that it carries out and ensures that the amount of capital and liquid assets it holds is sufficient to undertake an orderly wind-down. 

The following are the outcomes that were sought: 

- A prudential regime that is aligned to the way that investment firms run their business: 

   - taking into account the different business models of FCA investment firms, and 

   - better protecting consumers and markets from the harm these firms may pose. 

- FCA investment firms are subject to meaningful and consistent prudential requirements. 

- FCA investment firms spend less time on complex capital requirement calculations that do little to help them to manage risk: 

   - allowing a firm’s management more time to focus on running the business, managing and mitigating any harm and risk, and 

   - allowing the FCA to focus on how a firm is managing itself. 

- Prudential rules that are understandable and accessible, with most rules brought into a new single prudential sourcebook (MIFIDPRU). 

## 1.18.1 Sections of the FCA MIFIDPRU Sourcebook 

## Application (MIFIDPRU1) 

The FCA removed the current definitions of FCA investment firms in BIPRU, IFPRU and exempt-CAD. 

Rather, there will be two broad categories of FCA investment firm – a ‘small and non-interconnected’ investment (SNI) firm or a ‘non-SNI’ firm. 

The prudential requirements in the IFPR are designed to scale with the size and complexity of the firm. 

## **Prudential Consolidation and the Group Capital Test (MIFIDPRU2)** 

Prudential consolidation will apply where there is an investment form group that comprises of a UK parent undertaking and its subsidiaries. 

54 

Regulatory Framework 

## Own Funds Resources (MIFIDPRU3) 

The types and treatment of own funds under the UK IFPR will follow the same requirements as under the UK CRR (with some minor amendments) and will comprise the following three classes of capital: 

- Common Equity Tier 1 (or CET1) capital. 

- Additional Tier 1 (or AT1) capital. 

- Tier 2 (or T2) capital. 

## Own Funds Requirements (MIFIDPRU4) 

The own funds requirements of FCA investment firms are based on a permanent minimum capital requirement (‘PMR’) and requirements based on the activities and services that the firm undertakes. 

There are a number of transitional provisions to assist firms to adjust to the capital requirements of the UK IFPR – especially where there is a relatively large increase from their previous capital requirements. 

## Concentration Risk (MIFIDPRU5) 

General provisions on monitoring and control of concentration risk apply to all firms, with limited exemptions. 

Provisions on limits and own funds requirements for concentration risk (K-CON) only apply to firms with a trading book, ie, trading as principal. 

## Liquidity (MIFIDPRU6) 

The UK IFPR applies a basic liquid asset requirement for firms which must hold liquid assets. 

The FCA has noted that core liquid assets to be used to meet the basic liquid assets requirement must be denominated in sterling – as it considers that firms liquidity needs are in sterling. 

The basic liquidity requirements form part of the overall framework that a firm must adopt for assessing its individual liquidity needs – this framework is called the ‘Internal Capital and Risk Assessment (ICARA)’. 

## Risk Management and Governance, ICARA and SREP (MIFIDPRU7) 

The FCA approach is underpinned by the following principles: 

- Firms must consider and account for the risk of harm they pose to consumers and markets. 

- The ‘ICARA’ process is the centrepiece of a firm’s risk management processes. 

- Senior management is responsible for ensuring the appropriateness of the firm’s governance and risk management. 

- The FCA will intervene at given intervention points if firms fail to act appropriately, or their actions prove unsuccessful. 

- The FCA expectations on firms are proportionate to the risk of harm posed. 

55 

## Overall Financial Adequacy Rule (OFAR) 

Firms’ financial and non-financial resources must be appropriate to the regulated activities that they undertake. 

The requirements provide a link between financial resources required under the OIFPR and the FCA’s Threshold Conditions for permissions. 

Firms determine the OFAR through their ICARA process, which represents what is required to meet the OFAR, unless otherwise advised by the FCA. 

## Internal Capital Adequacy and Risk Assessment (ICARA) 

All firms will be required to undertake an ‘ICARA’, which should be monitored/updated on a continuous process. 

## Disclosure and ESG (MIFIDPRU8) 

The FCA sets out the disclosures that are required for both ‘SNI’ and ‘non-SNI’ firms. Disclosure will be required for: 

- risk management 

- own funds 

- own funds requirements 

- investment policy 

- governance requirements, and 

- remuneration. 

## Regulatory Reporting (MIFIDPRU9) 

The FCAs intention is to only collect data that will let it supervise firms against the requirements of the UK IFPR. 

Firms will be required to complete financial reporting on a quarterly basis. 

## 1.19 BREXIT – The Implications for the UK and Europe 

Brexit is the term used to define the UK’s exit from the EU, although the UK left the EU on 31 January 2020, it  remained subject to EU laws/rules until 31 December 2020. 

The European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 (‘EUWA’) gives the government powers to amend current legislation to ensure that it functions when the UK is no longer an EU member state. The intention is that the same rules and laws apply on the day after the exit as on the day before, as far as possible. 

Following Brexit, UK firms lost their ability to access the EU single market on the basis of ‘ **passporting** ’; this means that UK firms are limited in their ability to provide financial services to EU clients, likewise for EU firms to UK clients. 

56 

Regulatory Framework 

UK firms may look for exemptions in local law that allow for access by firms from non-EU countries or apply for permission from the European Commission, or other EU bodies under the access regimes for third countries provided in EU legislation. 

To explain what this means to existing EU regulations: 

- With respect to MiFIR, from 1 January 2021, the onshored (retained) ‘UK MiFIR’ legislation applies to all UK regulated firms. 

- UK MiFIR means the Regulation (EU) No 600/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 15 May 2014 on markets in financial instruments and amending Regulation (EU) No 648/2012 as it forms part of domestic law of the UK by virtue of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018. 

- UK MiFIR, together with the Markets in Financial Instruments Directive 2014/65/EU, as implemented in the UK by the FCA in its FCA rules (UK MiFID) ensure UK firms are complying with the same legislation as EU firms (although, over time, regulatory divergence will have an impact). 

However, there were some areas where the FCA expected firms to be compliant with the UK onshored version of EU Regulations from 31 December 2020, they were: 

- MiFID II Transaction Reporting 

- EMIR Reporting 

- SFTR Reporting 

- certain MAR requirements 

- CASS rules, and 

- market-making exemption under the Short Selling Rules (SSR). 

## 1.19.1 European Union (Withdrawal) Act 

The European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018 (‘EUWA’) created the ability for EU Regulations that were in existence before the Brexit transitional date of 31 December 2020, to be retained as primary legislation in UK law – and can only be updated by an Act of Parliament or any other primary legislation. 

The European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020 is an Act of the Parliament of the UK that makes legal provision for both the ratification of the Brexit Withdrawal Agreement and implements it into British domestic law. The Withdrawal Agreement was the result of Brexit negotiations. 

Onshoring, through the EUWA, provides a basis for directly applicable EU law to continue to be effective in the UK after Brexit. For financial services, this means that EMIR, MAR and MiFIR have been ‘onshored’ in the UK and are now part of UK law – they are referred to as UK regimes (‘UK EMIR’, ‘UK MiFIR’ and ‘UK MAR’). 

With regards to financial services, for the time being, the UK and the EU have the same regulations that apply – however, as noted in chapter 5 of the workbook, the UK is undertaking a review of financial services regulation now that the UK is no longer a member of the EU. 

57 

## 1.20 Financial Services and Markets Act (FSMA) 2023 

As noted by HM Treasury, the FSMA 2023 does: 

- implement the outcomes of the Future Regulatory Framework (FRF) Review 

   - this is now called the Smarter Regulatory Framework (SRF) 

- maintain the UK’s position as an open and global financial hub 

- harness the opportunities of innovative technologies in financial services 

- bolster the competitiveness of UK markets and promote the effective use of capital, and 

- support the levelling up agenda, promote financial inclusion and consumer protection. 

## _Source of information: HM Treasury_ 

The following are the sections of the Act: 

## **Part 1 (Regulatory Framework)** 

- Chapter 1 (revocation of EU laws) 

   - Revocation of retained EU Law 

- Chapter 2 (New regulatory powers) 

   - New regulatory powers (designated activities regime) - Section 8 

   - • Financial market infrastructure: general rules and requirements - Section 9 to 12 

   - • Financial market infrastructure: piloting powers - Sections 13 to 17 

   - • Powers in relation to critical third parties - Sections 18 and 19) 

   - • Financial Promotion - Section 20 

   - • Sustainability disclosure requirements - Section 21 

   - • Digital settlement assets - Sections 22 and 23 

   - • Mutual recognition - Section 24 

- Chapter 3 (accountability of regulators) 

• FCA and PRA objectives and regulatory principles - Sections 25 to 28 • FCA and PRA powers to make rules, etc - Sections 29 to 34 • FCA and PRA engagement - Sections 35 to 39 • Cooperation of FCA, PRA and others - Section 40 • FCA and PRA panels and policy statements 

   - Sections 41 to 47 

- Bank of England (BoE) regulatory powers 

   - Sections 48 to 50 

58 

Regulatory Framework 

   - Payment Systems Regulators powers 

      - Sections 51 and 52 

   - Consultation on rules 

      - Section 53 

   - Part 2 (Access to cash) 

      - Sections 54 and 55 

   - Part 3 (Performance of functions relating to financial market infrastructure) 

      - Section 56 

   - Part 4 (Central counterparties in financial difficulties) 

      - Section 57 

   - Part 5 (Insurers in financial difficulties) 

      - Section 58 

- Part 6 (Miscellaneous) 

   - Amendments to FSMA 2000 

      - Sections 59 to 69 

   - BoE levy 

      - Section 70 and 71 

   - Part 7 (General) 

      - Sections 80 to 87. 

## 2. International Financial Services Structure, Operation and Legislation 

This section of the chapter highlights the main EU legislation and laws (Regulations and **Directives** ) that are relevant to the UK Financial Services industry due to their application and scope being extraterritorial. 

A single market in financial services has long been an objective of the EU. In a single market, financial institutions authorised to provide financial services in one member state are able to provide the same services throughout the EU, competing on a level playing field within a consistent regulatory environment. 

From a financial services perspective, this should mean that, in terms of investor protection, it makes no difference to a customer whether they buy a financial product from a provider in their home, or from another member state. 

## 2.1 European Financial Services Structure, Operation and Legislation 

## 2.1.1 The European Commission (EC) 

The European Commission is independent of national governments, it represents and upholds the interests of the EU as a whole (collective). 

59 

Its main tasks are: 

- right of legislative initiative – all legislative decisions taken by the European Council have to be based on a proposal from the Commission. It acts as the engine of integration, drawing up proposals for the development of EU policy and building steps towards harmonisation 

- guardian of the treaties 

- executive authority for the implementation of EU policy, and 

- enacting technical standards drafted by ESMA. 

The EC is steered by a group of 27 Commissioners, one from each member state, known as ‘the College’. Together, they take or make decisions on the Commission’s political and strategic direction. A new College of Commissioners is appointed every five years. 

## 2.1.2 The European Parliament (EP) 

It is a multinational chamber Parliament, consisting of Members of European Parliament (MEPs) with seats allocated on the basis of population. The Parliament is supposed to express the democratic wishes of EU citizens and represents their interests in discussions with other EU institutions. MEPs sit in Europewide political groups rather than in national blocks. 

The MEP’s principal roles are: 

- examine and adopt European legislation, sharing this with the European Council (Council of the EU) 

- approve the EU budget, and 

- exercise democratic control over the other EU institutions by setting up committees of inquiry. 

## 2.1.3 The Council of the EU (the European Council) 

The European Council defines the EU’s overall political direction and priorities. It is not one of the EU’s legislating institutions, so does not negotiate or adopt EU laws (the Council of the EU and the EP are responsible for adopting laws proposed by the Commission). Each member state has a representative in the Council. 

## 2.1.4 EU Legislation 

Primary legislation consists of the various treaties which have been negotiated and ratified by the member states. 

Secondary legislation consists of regulations, directives, decisions, recommendations and opinions. All are binding with the exception of recommendations and opinions. Decisions can be addressed to both member states and/or individuals and businesses. Regulations are directly applicable and binding throughout the EU. They do not need to be transposed into national law; rather, member states are directly bound by such measures once they are implemented into EU law. 

60 

Regulatory Framework 

A directive is binding on member states in relation to the objectives to be achieved but gives member states discretion in determining how the legal measure should be incorporated into national and/or domestic law. 

## 2.2 Financial Supervision in Europe 

As part of the overall response to the financial crisis of 2007–08, the EU Commission enhanced the legal framework in relation to the oversight of financial services in the EU. The below framework has been operational since 2010. 

The Commission adopted an architecture that created the following: 

- **European Systemic Risk Council or Board (ESRB)** , to assess the risks to the stability of the financial system as a whole and provide early warning of any systemic risks and, where necessary, recommendations for action. 

- **European System of Financial Supervisors** , to supervise financial institutions, which consists of a network of national financial supervisors working with three **European Supervisory Authorities (ESAs)** : 

   - banking (European Banking Authority – EBA) 

   - securities (European Securities and Markets Authority –ESMA), and 

   - insurance and occupational pensions sectors (European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority – EIOPA. 

The ESRB has three main objectives. They are: 

- to develop a European macroprudential perspective to address the problem of fragmented individual risk analysis at a national level 

- to enhance the effectiveness of early warning mechanisms by improving the interaction between microprudential analysis, and 

- to allow for risk assessments to be translated into action by the relevant authorities. 

The objectives of the ESAs are to contribute to: 

- improving the functioning of the internal market, which includes a high, effective and consistent level of regulation and supervision 

- protecting depositors, investors, policyholders and other beneficiaries 

- ensuring the integrity, efficiency and orderly functioning of financial markets 

- safeguarding the stability of the financial system, and 

- strengthening international supervisory coordination. 

## 2.2.1 Implementation and Enforcement 

## Level 1 

- Framework legislation is proposed by the Commission and adopted by the Council and the EP. The directive or regulation specifies whether legislative power is delegated to the Commission to adopt Level 2 measures. 

61 

## Level 2 

- Secondary legislation is drafted and adopted by the Commission in line with the delegation of legislative competence in the Level 1 text. 

- This is done by four specialist committees of finance ministry experts. These are the European Banking Committee (EBC), the European Securities Committee (ESC), the European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Committee (EIOPC) and the European Financial Conglomerates Committee (EFCC). 

- The Commission may seek advice from the relevant ESA on implementing measures. 

- The ESAs would advise on detailed Level 2 requirements. 

## Level 3 

- The ESAs are empowered to adopt guidance, and/or guidelines, which is treated on a ‘comply or explain’ basis by national supervisors. The ESAs are required to consult and undertake cost/benefit analysis on this ‘where appropriate’. 

- These are commonly produced in the form of ‘Q&As’ or opinion letters/statements. 

## Level 4 Enforcement 

- The Commission is responsible for ensuring that directives are properly transposed and that EU legal requirements are then applied, undertaking enforcement action where necessary. 

## 2.3 The European Supervisory Authorities (ESAs) 

The main purpose of the ESAs is to create a single EU rulebook, by developing draft technical standards which will be adopted by the EU Commission. 

A key change in the introduction of the ESAs is that they have much stronger powers, having the ability to bind the member states in ways that their predecessors did not, with the trend being towards greater EU control. In the frequently asked questions (FAQs) section of its website, ESMA states: 

_‘ESMA’s work on securities legislation contributes to the development of a single rule book in Europe…’_ 

The FAQs also state: 

_‘As part of its role in standard-setting and reducing the scope of regulatory arbitrage, ESMA strengthens international supervisory cooperation. Where requested in European law, ESMA undertakes the supervision of certain entities with pan-European reach_ .’ 

The ESAs also issue guidance, and/or guidelines, including recommendations with which national supervisors and firms must make every effort to comply, or explain why they are not able to ensure compliance with the requirements. 

The ESAs have the power to investigate a national supervisory authority if they believe that the national authority is either failing to apply EU law, or is doing so in a way which appears to be in breach of EU law (or not in the spirit of the guidance, and/or guidelines which the EU has provided). Following an investigation, the ESA may issue a recommendation to the national supervisory authority, as well as obtaining formal opinion from the Commission. It is at this stage that the ESA will take appropriate decisions, binding on firms or market participants to ensure they comply with EU law. 

62 

Regulatory Framework 

ESAs can temporarily ban certain financial activities. 

## 2.3.1 European Banking Authority (EBA) 

The European Banking Authority (EBA) is composed of high-level representatives from the banking supervisory authorities and central banks of the European Union. Their role is to: 

- advise the EU Commission, either at the Commission’s request, within a time limit which the Commission may lay down according to the urgency of the matter, or on the Committee’s own initiative; in particular, with regards to the preparation of draft-implementing measures in the field of banking activities, and 

- contribute to the consistent implementation of Community directives and to the convergence of member states’ supervisory practices throughout the Community, enhancing supervisory cooperation, including the exchange of information. 

## 2.3.2 European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority (EIOPA) 

The European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority (EIOPA) is an independent advisory body to the EP and the Council of the EU. EIOPA’s core responsibilities are to support the stability of the financial system, including the transparency of markets and financial products, as well as the protection of insurance policyholders, pension scheme members and beneficiaries. 

## 2.3.3 European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) 

The European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) is an independent authority which helps underpin the stability of the EU’s financial system by ensuring the integrity, transparency, efficiency and orderly functioning of securities markets, as well as enhancing investor protection. In particular, ESMA encourages supervisory convergence both among securities regulators, and across financial sectors by working with the other ESAs. 

As noted on the ESMA website, it contributes to the development of a single rulebook in Europe. This: 

- ensures the consistent treatment of investors across the EU, enabling an adequate level of protection, and 

- promotes equal conditions for the competition of financial service providers, as well as ensuring the effectiveness and cost-efficiency of supervision for supervised companies. 

ESMA also contributes to the financial stability of the EU, identifying potential risks and providing advice. ESMA is also responsible for coordinating the actions of securities supervisors (with each EEA member state) and adopting emergency measures in a crisis. 

While ESMA is independent, it is fully accountable to the European Parliament, where it will appear before the relevant committee, known as the Committee on Economic and Monetary Affairs (ECON), at its request, and for formal hearings. ESMA is also accountable to the European Council and the European Commission. ESMA reports on its activities regularly at meetings, but also through an annual report. 

63 

ESMA’s competencies and powers include: 

- a supervisory role in relation to credit rating agencies 

- drafting legally binding technical standards in EU member states 

- launching a fast-track procedure to ensure consistent application of EU law 

- participation in Colleges of Supervisors and on-site inspections 

- resolving disagreements between national authorities 

- consumer protection responsibilities, including the ability to prohibit, for a period of three months, financial products which in its view pose a threat to financial stability or the orderly functioning of financial markets 

- entering into administrative arrangements with supervisory authorities, international organisations and the administrations of third countries, and 

- monitoring systemic risk of cross-border financial institutions. 

Level 1 directives and regulations continue to set out the high-level political objectives and goals in the area concerned by the legislation. ESMA now has a bigger role in Level 2 in relation to the drafting of regulatory technical standards (delegated acts and implementing acts). Delegated acts relate to the substantive content of the legislative requirement, ie, setting out what authorisation information firms must provide to competent authorities, while implementing acts give effect to the substantive requirements. This includes standard templates and procedures for communicating information between competent authorities. 

ESMA will develop guidelines and recommendations at Level 3 to ensure a consistent and effective supervisory approach. These guidelines and recommendations are addressed to competent authorities and/or financial market participants. While they are not legally binding, competent authorities must explain if they do not intend to comply with such requirements, and regulated firms would need to be able to explain to their competent authorities why they are not complying with the Level 3 requirements. 

At Level 4, ESMA can, by a national competent authority, the EP, the Council, the Commission or by a relevant stakeholder group, be requested to launch an inquiry, or issue a recommendation addressed to the national authority. The Commission can also follow its usual procedures for referring a case against a member state to the EU Court of Justice. 

## 2.4 The European Central Bank (ECB) 

The ECB is the central bank for Europe’s single currency, the ‘euro’. The ECB’s main task is to maintain the euro’s purchasing power, and thus price stability in the eurozone area. The euro area comprises of the 20 EU countries which have adopted the euro since 1999. 

The ECB and the national central banks together constitute the Eurosystem, the central banking system of the eurozone area. The main objective of the Eurosystem is to maintain price stability, safeguarding the value of the euro. The ECB is committed to performing all central bank tasks entrusted to it effectively. In doing so, it strives for the highest level of integrity, competence, efficiency and transparency. 

64 

Regulatory Framework 

## 2.4.1 Why a System Instead of a Single Central Bank? 

There are three main reasons for having a system of central banking in Europe: 

1. To build on the existing competencies of the NCBs, their institutional set-up, infrastructure, expertise and excellent operational capabilities. Moreover, several central banks perform tasks which are in addition to those of the Eurosystem. 

2. It is appropriate for credit institutions to have an access point to central banking facilities in each participating member state. 

3. Given the multitude of nations, languages and cultures in the euro area, NCBs are best placed to serve as access points of the Eurosystem. 

## 2.4.2 Objective of the ECB 

The objective of the ECB is to maintain price stability, in doing so to protect the value of the euro. 

The Eurosystem comprises the ECB and the national central banks of the other ‘euro areas’ member states, with responsibility for: 

- defining and implementing monetary policy 

- conducting foreign exchange operations 

- holding and managing the euro area’s foreign currency reserves, and 

- promoting the smooth operation of payment systems. 

In addition, the ECB also undertakes additional tasks in the areas of banking supervisions, banknotes, statistics, macroprudential policy and financial stability, as well as international and European cooperation. 

## 2.5 The Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID II) and Markets in Financial Instruments Regulation (MiFIR) 

MiFID provisions came into force in 2007. One of the key aims of MiFID was to provide investor protection rules across the EEA. Investor protection is ensured, _inter alia,_ via the obligation to obtain the best possible result for the client, information disclosure requirements, client-specific rules on suitability and appropriateness and rules on inducements. As a general principle, MiFID placed significant importance on the fiduciary duties of firms. 

This is why MiFID established a general obligation for firms to act in the client’s best interest. MiFID was designed to support two key policy goals of the EU, which at the time were: 

- extending the scope of the passport rules within the EEA to include a wider range of services, and 

- removing a major hurdle to cross-border business by the application of **home state** rules. 

As noted below, MiFID was repealed and replaced by a new Directive (MiFID II) and a Regulation (MiFIR) in 2018. 

Within the workbook, reference to ‘MiFID’ means MiFID II and MiFIR. 

65 

Further enhancements were made to MiFID. In 2024 the revised MiFID regime (MiFID 3 and MiFIR 2) was published, with MiFIR 2 coming into effect in 2024 (unless Level 2 measures are required to be developed by ESMA), while EU member states must transpose MiFID 3 into national law by the end of September 2025. 

## 2.5.1 Activities that can be Passported under MiFID 

## MiFID Activity 

- Receipt and transmission of orders in relation to one or more financial instruments. 

- Execution of orders on behalf of clients. 

- Dealing on own account. 

- Portfolio management. 

- Investment advice. 

- Underwriting of financial instruments and/or placing of financial instruments on a firm commitment basis. 

- Placing of financial instruments without a firm commitment basis. 

- Operation of MTFs. 

- Operation of OTFs. 

## Ancillary Activities 

The following services cannot be passported in their own right; they can only be passported if they are being provided in conjunction with a core investment service from the above list. 

## **MiFID Ancillary Activity** 

- Safekeeping and administration of financial instruments for the accounts of clients, including custodianship. Also related services such as the management of cash and collateral. 

- Granting credits or loans to an investor to allow them to carry out a transaction in one or more financial instruments when the firm granting the credit or loan is involved in the transaction. 

- Advice to undertakings on capital structure, industrial strategy and related matters; also, advice, and/or services relating to mergers and the purchase of undertakings. 

- Foreign exchange services (but only if these are connected with the provision of investment services). 

- Investment research and financial analysis, or other forms of general recommendation in relation to transactions in financial instruments. 

- Services in relation to underwriting. 

- Investment services and activities, and ancillary services, related to the underlying assets of certain derivatives when these are connected to the provision of investment or ancillary services. 

## 2.5.2 Financial Instruments Covered by MiFID 

MiFID applies only to activities in relation to a specified list of financial instruments. These are: 

- transferable securities 

- money market instruments 

- units in collective investment undertakings 

66 

Regulatory Framework 

- options, futures, swaps, forward rate agreements and any other derivative contracts relating to securities, currencies, interest rates or yields, emission allowances or other derivatives instruments, financial indices or financial measures which may be settled physically or in cash 

- options, futures, swaps, forwards and any other derivative contracts relating to commodities that must be settled in cash or may be settled in cash at the option of one of the parties other than by reason of default or other termination event 

- options, futures, swaps, and any other derivative contract relating to commodities that can be physically settled providing that they are traded on a regulated market, an MTF, or an OTF, except for wholesale energy products traded on an OTF that must be physically settled 

- options, futures, swaps, forwards and any other derivative contracts relating to commodities that can be physically settled not otherwise mentioned in point 6 of this section and not being for commercial purposes, which have the characteristics of other derivative financial instruments 

- derivative instruments for the transfer of credit risk 

- financial contracts for differences (CFDs) 

- options, futures, swaps, forward rate agreements and any other derivative contracts relating to climatic variables, freight rates or inflation rates or other official economic statistics that must be settled in cash or may be settled in cash at the option of one of the parties other than by reason of default or other termination event, as well as any other derivative contracts relating to assets, rights, obligations, indices and measures not otherwise mentioned in this section, which have the characteristics of other derivative financial instruments, having regard to whether, _inter alia_ , they are traded on a regulated market, OTF, or an MTF, and 

- emission allowances consisting of any units recognised for compliance with the requirements of Directive 2003/87/EC (Emissions Trading Scheme). 

Financial instruments not covered by MiFID include the following: 

- Foreign Exchange (FX spot and the conversion related to settlement of a security where the settlement date is greater than two business days). 

- However, this would not include where it relates to the provision of an investment activity or service (ie, entering into an FX forward contract or an FX option or future). 

## 2.5.3 MiFID – Home versus Host State Regulation 

The concept of **passporting** under MiFID relies on the concept of home state and **host state** regulators. In essence, the home state is where the firm carrying out the activities is established; the host state is the state in which provides services as a guest. 

MiFID tackles the hurdle to a single market in the following ways: 

- It allows firms to carry out cross-border business from their home state, solely on the basis of their home country rules. 

- It harmonises these home country rules so that they are all sufficiently similar, meaning that an investor is, in theory, just as well-protected under the rules of one EEA member state as they are under the rules of another. There are common standards of investor protection. 

- Host state conduct of business rules apply where a passported MiFID branch of a firm conducts business with host state residents.
