**==> picture [219 x 679] intentionally omitted <==**

## ~~**Overall Learning Objective**~~ 

To demonstrate a knowledge and understanding of the FCA’s and PRA’s rules and guidance as applicable to investment business conducted in the UK. 

## 1. Regulatory Structure in the UK 

HM Treasury has overall responsibility for the UK’s financial system, the institutional structure of financial regulation and the legislation that governs it. 

The Financial Services Act 2012 enacted the split of the then regulator, the Financial Services Authority (FSA), replacing it with three regulatory authorities – the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA), the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) and the Financial Policy Committee (FPC). 

- The **FPC** – part of the Bank of England (BoE), with responsibility for macroprudential regulation, or regulation of the stability and resilience of the financial system as a whole. 

- • The **PRA** – regulating deposit takers (ie, banks), insurers and systemically important investment firms. This is also part of the BoE. 

- The **FCA** – regulating conduct issues across the entire spectrum of financial services. It is responsible for market supervision, as well as the prudential supervision of firms not supervised by the PRA (ie, solo regulated firms). The FCA is accountable to HM Treasury. 

91 

## 1.1 The Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) 

The PRA’s role is to contribute to the promotion of the stability of the UK financial system through microprudential regulation of credit institutions (banks/deposit takers), credit unions, insurance companies and significantly important investment firms. It has an overall objective to promote the safety and soundness of regulated firms, it meets this objective primarily by seeking to minimise any adverse effects of firm failure on the UK financial system, and by ensuring that firms carry on their business in a way that avoids adverse effects on the system. 

For insurance supervision, the PRA has two complementary objectives – to secure an appropriate degree of protection for policyholders and, as needed, to minimise the adverse impact that the failure of an insurer or the way it carries out its business could have on the stability of the system. 

The PRA supervises around 1,500 banks, building societies, credit unions, insurers and major investment banks. 

## 1.2 The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) 

The FCA follows an approach of credible deterrence, pursuing enforcement cases to punish wrongdoing. Its market regulation continues to promote integrity and carry on the fight against market misconduct and insider dealing. 

The FCA’s strategic objective is to ensure that financial services markets function well. 

To ensure that the relevant markets work well, the FCA focuses on delivering good market conduct. The FCA’s key priorities in this respect are: 

- a renewed focus on wholesale conduct – in particular inherent conflicts of interest 

- trust in the integrity of markets, and 

- preventing market abuse. 

The National Audit Office is the statutory auditor of the FCA, with the power to carry out value-formoney reviews. HM Treasury is also able to carry out economic, efficiency and effectiveness reviews. The FCA is directly accountable to HM Treasury, but ultimately to Parliament. 

The FCA is primarily responsible for undertaking investigations and enforcement action in relation to financial crime. The FCA sees ‘credible deterrence’ as using enforcement as a regulatory tool – selecting cases to enforce against to send a strong and clear message to other firms not to engage in wrongful activity. 

Powers provided by the Financial Services Act 2012 mean that the FCA can publicly announce that it has begun disciplinary action against a firm or an individual – however, they have to date not used this power due to the complications and unintended consequences that this announcement could entail. The FCA is able to publish details of a warning notice proposing disciplinary action, to signal the start of formal enforcement proceedings. However, the FCA must consult the recipient of the warning notice before publishing. 

92 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

Following the coming into effect of FSMA 2023, the PRA, along with the FCA, have a secondary objective to: 

_‘facilitate the international competitiveness of the UK economy and its medium-to long-term growth (subject to international standards)’._ 

## 1.3 FCA Strategic and Operational Objectives, Statutory Objectives, Structure, Powers and Activities 

Sitting underneath the FCA’s strategic objective are their operational objectives – which are to: 

- protect consumers from bad conduct from regulated firms 

- maintain market integrity (protecting the integrity of the UK financial system), and 

- promote effective competition in the interest of consumers. 

Underlining the operational objectives are the following broad outcomes – which are embedded into the Consumer Duty (refer to section 6 of this chapter): 

- Consumers have access to fair products and services, which deliver what they promise. 

- Consumers can be confident that firms treat them fairly and fix problems promptly. 

- Competition contributes to improved consumer outcomes. 

- Firms compete on clear costs and consumers have the information they need. 

- Consumers can trust firms to be fit and proper and for financial markets to be clean. 

- A respected regulatory system that enables firms to assess and measure their level of compliance. 

The FCA carries out in-depth structured supervision work with those firms with the potential to cause the greatest risks to their objectives. This means fewer supervisors allocated to specific firms – but allows the greater flexibility to carry out more reviews on products and issues across a particular sector or market-thematic reviews. This new approach is underpinned by judgement-based supervision. 

The FCA’s integrity objective includes within it the ‘soundness, stability and resilience’ of the UK financial system. With regard to resilience, they expect firms to operate high standards in their risk management, having procedures in place to ensure continuity of critical services. Firms are required to comply with standards for resilience and recovery established in this area. 

To ensure that the relevant markets work well, the FCA has increased its focus on delivering good market conduct outcomes. 

The thinking behind ‘outcomes focused’ supervision is that, previously, there was too much focus and attention paid to ‘box-ticking’ and ‘principles-based regulation’ in supervision. The focus now is around good supervision, based on judgement and outcomes, the art of looking at a situation from several angles and seeking to identify weaknesses. 

The FCA’s approach to supervision is incorporated into its Supervision Sourcebook, in SUP1A.1. 

93 

The FCA will make supervisory judgements about a firm’s business model and forward-looking strategy, and intervening when it sees unacceptable risks to the fair treatment of customers. It will look for firms to base their business model, their culture, and how they run their business on a foundation of the Consumer Duty and/or the Fair Treatment of Customers (FTOC) (where applicable) in delivering desired outcomes for customers – retail and professional clients. 

To make judgement-based supervision work, the FCA needs staff who have strong, relevant skills, and experience. From an FCA perspective, conduct regulation, which is essentially about how people behave, is different from prudential regulation which can be more of an analytical exercise. Therefore, this requires a different skill set. 

This approach means that FCA supervisors will conduct in-depth and structured supervisory work on those firms with the greatest potential to cause risk to consumers, market integrity or competition. Some firms may experience a higher intensity of supervision than others. 

The Financial Services Act 2012 gave the FCA powers to help it achieve its outcomes. One of the important powers is the ability to ban financial products that pose unacceptable risks to consumers, including publishing details of misleading financial promotions, and letting people know when the FCA is proposing to take disciplinary action against a firm. 

## 1.3.1 Risk-Based Approach 

The FCA takes a proportionate, judgement-based and outcomes approach. It assesses the risk a firm poses to its objectives, either individually or collectively, and then focuses its resources on managing the risk issue most likelihood to crystallise. 

In summary, the FCA: 

- identifies and assesses risks (both emerging and current) to consumers, firms and financial markets 

- identifies the risk of market failures hindering effective competition 

- develops a general understanding of the risks and issues in the financial markets to support its policy-making, authorisation, supervision and enforcement functions 

- makes evidence-based policies to help change firm and market behaviour 

- prioritises, manages and mitigates risk consistently, and uses a risk-based approach for making decisions 

- establishes common standards and principles for measuring and assessing risk across the organisation, and 

- puts in place the infrastructure, systems and tools to catalogue, analyse and assess risk. 

## 1.3.2 Secure an Appropriate Degree of Protection for Consumers 

The FCA expects firms to have their customers at the heart of how they do business, when they develop, and promote or sell products and services. Therefore, the FCA’s aims and objectives are to: 

- ensure that customers are treated in a way that is appropriate for their level of financial knowledge and understanding 

- be more outward-looking, by engaging more with consumers and understanding more about their concerns and behaviour 

94 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

- set clear expectations for firms and be clear about what they can expect from the FCA 

- intervene early to tackle potential risks to consumers before they occur, and 

- maintain a strategy of ‘credible deterrence’, including intervening earlier, and continuing to hold senior individuals to account. 

## 1.3.3 Protect and Enhance the Integrity of the UK Financial System 

The FCA seeks to develop and support a healthy and successful financial system, where financial markets are efficient and transparent, firms can thrive and consumers can place their trust in transparent and open markets. This means that the markets need to be supported by appropriate infrastructure, access and transparency to meet the needs of the consumers, corporates and other wholesale clients that use them. 

To achieve this, they seek to ensure that: 

- senior management are accountable for their firm’s activities, including principal and agency responsibilities in the wholesale markets 

- there is a positive culture of proactively identifying and managing conflicts of interest 

- there is orderly resolution and return of client assets 

- firms’ business models, activities, controls and behaviours maintain trust in the integrity of markets and do not create or allow market abuse, systemic risk or financial crime 

- market efficiency, cleanliness and resilience are delivered through transparency, surveillance and the supervision of infrastructures, as well as their principal users 

- firms, acting as agents on behalf of their clients, put clients’ best interests at the heart of their businesses, and 

- early intervention in wholesale markets to mitigate the risk of harm being transmitted to retail consumers. 

## 1.3.4 Promote Effective Competition in the Interests of Consumers 

The FCA applies a holistic approach to assessing whether competition in markets is working well for consumers. In assessing whether intervention is needed, it considers a range of market failures including: 

- unilateral market power 

- barriers to entry and expansion 

- coordinated conduct by firms 

- vertical relationships 

- weak customer response, and 

- principal-agent problems. 

As noted above, the FCA’s aim and objective is to be a proactive force in enabling the right outcomes for consumers and market participants. 

95 

In addition to fulfilling its stated objectives, the FCA is responsible for: 

- the conduct regulation of all firms, covering the range of their dealings with retail customers, through to their activities in wholesale markets; it regulates about 42,000 firms in total, including those prudentially supervised by the PRA 

- the prudential supervision of all firms not prudentially supervised by the PRA (approximately 41,000) 

- setting specific rules for around 17,000 firms 

- supervision of trading infrastructure including the investment exchanges and over-the-counter (OTC) markets and monitoring firms’ compliance with the market abuse regime 

- investigating and prosecuting insider dealing through the use of its criminal powers 

- acting as the UK Listing Authority, and 

- overseeing the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) and the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS), jointly with the PRA. 

The FCA has increased its focus on the conduct of firms. Firms’ senior management set the culture of their organisation, so the FCA is looking to ensure that what is decided and agreed by senior management translates to good outcomes for consumers. 

The Policy, Risk and Research Division within the FCA acts as the radar of the organisation. It combines research into what is happening in the market and to consumers with better analysis of the type of risks and where they appear. 

In summary the division: 

- identifies and assesses risks to consumers, firms and markets – both emerging and current 

- creates a common view of the risks in financial markets to inform the FCA’s authorisation, supervision and enforcement decisions, and 

- uses the knowledge of these risks to make evidence-based policy that changes behaviour. 

By bringing together the information gathering, analysis, research and policy-making into one place, the FCA is better placed to make timely and effective interventions where risks have been identified. 

## 1.4 PRA Strategic and Operational Objectives, Structure, Powers and Activities 

In addition to its statutory objectives, noted above in section 1.1, the PRA has eight fundamental rules that firms must comply with. 

Therefore, firms regulated by the PRA must: 

1. conduct its business with integrity 

2. conduct its business with due skill, care and diligence 

3. act in a prudent manner 

4. maintain adequate financial resources at all times 

5. have effective risk strategies and risk management systems 

6. organise and control its affairs responsibly and effectively 

96 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

7. deal with its regulators in an open and cooperative way, and must disclose to the PRA appropriately anything relating to the firm of which the PRA would reasonably expect notice, and 

8. prepare for resolution so, if the need arises, it can be resolved in an orderly manner with a minimum disruption of critical services. 

There is a key difference between the PRA’s statutory objectives and its fundamental rules. The objectives are applied to the PRA, and they establish what the PRA is to achieve as a regulator. The fundamental rules are applied by the PRA, setting out the expected rules to be adhered to by firms regulated by it. 

The PRA achieves its statutory objectives by: 

- seeking to ensure that any firms that fail, do so in a way that avoids significant disruption to the supply of critical financial services 

- emphasising resolution planning to permit orderly failure 

- cooperating closely with the FPC and the FCA to ensure macro and microprudential regulation is aligned across the markets, and 

- working with the FCA and others to ensure the UK authorities have a strong voice in global policy making. 

The PRA’s approach to supervision relies significantly on the judgement of its staff. The PRA supervises firms and assesses whether they are financially safe and sound, that they meet, and are likely to continue to meet, the threshold conditions. It looks not just at current risks, but also against those that could manifest in the future. 

The PRA focuses on those issues and those firms that pose the greatest risk to the stability of the UK financial system. The frequency and intensity of the supervision experienced by firms increase in line with the risks that they pose to the stability of the financial services sector. 

The PRA focuses on material issues when engaging with firms. 

As the PRA’s approach is based significantly on the judgement of its staff, it relies on experienced staff and detailed analysis. 

The PRA utilises a risk framework, which captures three key elements: 

- The potential impact that a firm could have on financial stability in the UK, both by the way it carries out its business and in the event of failure. 

- How the external context in which a firm operates, and the business risks it faces (together, its risk context) might affect the viability of the firm. 

- Mitigating factors including: a firm’s management and governance and its risk management and controls (operational mitigation); its financial strength, specifically capital and liquidity (financial mitigation), and its resolvability (structural mitigation). 

97 

## 1.4.1 Enforcement Powers 

The PRA’s preference is to use its powers to secure up front remedial action, given its approach of intervening early to address emerging risks. However, it does have the authority and powers to impose financial penalties and publish censures in cases where sanctions are inappropriate. It also has powers when directions or restrictions imposed by the PRA are ignored by the firm. 

The PRA deploys its disciplinary powers as follows: 

- Reinforcing the PRA’s objectives and priorities. 

- Promoting high standards of regulatory behaviour. 

- The need to send a clear signal to a firm (and to the regulated community), about the circumstances in which the PRA considers a firm’s behaviour to be unacceptable. 

- Deterring future misconduct. 

## 1.4.2 BoE Enforcement Decision Making Committee (EDMC) 

The Enforcement Decision Making Committee (EDMC) is a committee of the BoE. It helps the BoE to discharge its responsibilities and strengthen its enforcement process by ensuring that there is a functional separation between the investigation teams and the decision makers in contested enforcement cases. 

Members of the EDMC will be independent of the BoE’s executive team and will not be employees of the BoE. They will be appointed by the Court of the BoE and will serve for a term of three years. They will not be permitted to serve more than two three-year fixed terms. 

The EDMC will meet as often as necessary to discharge its functions; a panel of three members will be required to convene to hear and resolve each contested enforcement case and will have one vote on the matter under consideration. If the votes are split, then the panel lead hearing the case will have the casting vote. 

## Warning Notices 

In contested enforcement cases, if the BoE considers that a warning notice is appropriate, then it will make a recommendation to the EDMC, who will make the decision and will also determine the wording of the warning notice. 

## 1.4.3 Structure 

The PRA’s approach is advanced primarily by its frontline supervisors. Its judgements are grounded in analysis, supervisory experience and a strong understanding of the sectors it supervises, gained through direct exposure with the senior management of firms. The supervisors are supported by risk specialists. 

The structure of the PRA is such that the chief executive officer (CEO) is supported by three business areas: 

- Banking: 

   - domestic UK banks 

98 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

   - international UK banks 

   - investment banks and overseas banks, and 

   - risk specialists. 

- Insurance: 

   - general insurance, and 

   - life insurance. 

- Policy: 

   - banking policy, and 

   - insurance policy. 

## 1.4.4 Approach to Supervision – PRA 

The PRA’s approach relies significantly on judgement. The PRA supervises firms to judge whether they are safe and sound; and whether they meet, and are likely to continue to meet, the threshold conditions. PRA supervisors therefore reach judgements on the risks that a firm poses to the PRA’s objective, and how these can be addressed. 

The PRA has a statutory objective to promote the safety and soundness of firms. It is required to pursue this primarily by seeking to avoid adverse effects on financial stability, and in particular by seeking to minimise adverse effects resulting from disruption to the continuity of financial services that can be caused by the way firms run their business, or upon their failure. 

Therefore, the PRA’s key objective is focusing on those issues and the firms that pose the greatest risk to the stability of the UK financial system. The frequency and intensity of supervision applied by the PRA to a particular firm will increase in line with the risk that they pose to its overarching objective. 

The PRA’s supervisory judgements are, therefore, based on evidence and analysis. It is inherent in a forward-looking system that at times the supervisor’s judgement will be at odds with that of the firm. Furthermore, there will be occasions when events will show that the supervisor’s judgement, in hindsight, was wrong. 

The PRA’s regulatory decision-making is rigorous and well-documented, consistent with public law. Its most significant supervisory judgements are taken by the Prudential Regulation Committee (PRC). The PRC board is involved in the most important decisions on general policy and individual cases. 

Although, it should be remembered that both the PRA and the FCA do not operate a ‘no-fail’ regime with regards to the supervision of firms. 

## 1.5 Approach to Supervision – FCA 

The FCA defines ‘supervision’ as the continuing oversight of regulated firms and of individuals controlling these firms, to reduce actual and potential harm to consumers and markets. 

The FCA adopts a ‘pre-emptive’ approach based on making forward-looking judgments about firms business models, product strategy and how they run their businesses, in order for the FCA to identify, determine and intervene earlier to prevent problems arising. 

99 

Therefore, the FCA’s approach to the supervision of firms contributes to the delivery of the statutory objective to protect and enhance the integrity of the UK financial system. 

Where the FCA has responsibilities for prudential supervision, its focus will be on the reduction of impacts to customers and the integrity of the financial system where firms fail. In addition, when consumer detriment occurs, they will seek redress for impacted consumers. 

Underpinning the FCA’s work for supervision are supervisory principles; these guide the FCA’s work to protect consumers, enhance market integrity and promote competition and form the basis of its supervisory approach. 

The supervisory principles are complementary to the **Principles for Businesses** which outline the FCA’s expectations of firms. 

- 

## **Forward looking** 

   - Pre-empt poor conduct so that the risk does not crystallise, or where it does happen that the impact does not cause significant harm to markets or consumers. 

- **Focus on strategy and business models** 

   - Assessment of firms’ business models and strategies to identify potential emerging risk of harm. 

   - Have a good understanding of a firm’s business model, activities it undertakes and the risk to markets and consumers that this could pose. 

- **Focus on culture and governance** 

   - Assess the drivers of behaviour within firms. 

- **Focus on individuals as well as firm accountability** 

   - Hold to account senior individuals who manage and/or direct the firm with respect to its strategy. 

- 

## **Proportionate and risk-based** 

   - Use knowledge and understanding of markets that the FCA regulates to identify and target firms whose misconduct could cause harm. 

- **Two-way communication** 

   - Engagement with regulated firms and consumers. 

   - Clear on the behaviour expected of firms, the good and poor practice that is observed. 

- **Coordinated** 

   - Supervision teams work closely with other FCA departments. 

   - Work with other UK and international regulatory bodies. 

- **Put right systematic harm that has occurred and prevent it from happening again** 

   - Move quickly to prevent harm happening to markets and/or consumers. 

   - Where appropriate refer action/activities to the Enforcement division. 

_Source of information: FCA Website (SUP 1A.3.2): https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/SUP.pdf_ 

The FCA supervision model risk assessment process applies to all firms, although detailed requirements will vary between firms – some firms will have a highly intensive level of contact and others less so. This depends on the risk that they pose to the FCA’s strategic and operational objectives. 

100 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

The supervision model is based on three types of work (‘pillars’): 

- **Proactive** 

   - preventative work through structured conduct assessment of firms 

   - this includes model analysis and the drivers of culture 

- **Reactive** 

   - dealing with problems that are emerging or have crystallised, and securing customer redress or other remedial work (eg, to secure the integrity of the market) where necessary, and 

- **Thematic** 

   - looking at sectors of the market or products within a sector that are putting or may put consumers at risk 

   - wider diagnostic or remedy work where there is actual or potential harm across a number of firms or a products/service offering. 

## Supervision by Portfolio 

The FCA supervises most firms as members of a ‘portfolio’ of firms that share a common business model. The FCA analyses each portfolio and agrees a strategy to take action on firms posing the greatest risk – communicating this with firms on their expectations, priorities and examples of good or poor practice. 

## Dedicated Supervision Teams 

The FCA has a dedicated supervision team that monitors and engages with firms with the most likely potential of harm that could have an impact on consumers and markets. This team has a view of the whole firm across all sectors it operates in; it assesses the potential harm that the firm may cause and agrees a strategy to reduce or prevent it before it crystallises. 

## 1.5.1 FCA Prudential Supervision 

Although the PRA has prudential responsibility for bank/deposit takers (credit institutions), insurers and significantly important investment banks, the FCA is the prudential supervisor for a wide range of firms across the financial services sector, such as investment firms, asset managers, independent financial advisers, mortgage and insurance brokers – all those not supervised by the PRA (FCA solo-regulated firms). 

The FCA’s approach aims to minimise the harm to consumers, wholesale market participants and market stability when firms experience financial stress or fail in a disorderly manner. 

## Prudential Categories 

Firms that are prudentially regulated by the FCA fall into three prudential categories: P1, P2 and P3. These categories reflect the impact that the disorderly failure of a firm could have on markets and consumers. 

- **P1** – firms whose failure would cause lasting and widespread financial and reputational damage to their customers, client assets, the marketplace and beyond. These are subjected to periodic capital and, if applicable, liquidity assessment. The FCA’s prudential specialists conduct these tests every 24 months. 

101 

- **P2** – firms whose disorderly failure would damage consumers and client assets but are more easily dealt with than the failure of a P1 firm. These are subjected to periodic capital and, if applicable, liquidity assessment. The FCA’s prudential specialists conduct these tests every 48 months. 

- **P3** – firms whose failure, even if disorderly, would be unlikely to cause significant harm to consumers or market integrity. The FCA supervises these firms on a reactive basis. 

Like the conduct categories, the prudential categories determine the intensity of their prudential supervision for each firm. Through prudential activities the FCA seeks to ensure that: 

- firms maintain adequate financial resources in line with legal requirements 

- the FCA has an early warning system for financial issues that could drive behaviours that endanger a firm’s compliance with conduct, financial crime, client assets and other core regulatory standards 

- any wind-up of a firm could happen with little or no damage to markets and consumers. 

## Prudential Risk Analysis for P1 and P2 Firms 

## **Financial Resources Requirements (FRR)** 

The **FCA Handbook** specifies minimum **Financial Resources Requirements (FRR)** for all firms. Firms must maintain sufficient resources to meet their financial obligations at all times, and must be able to show how they have determined what is sufficient. This is the starting point for any prudential supervisory review. 

The specific scope and nature of FRR varies by firm type and permissions: 

- P1 firms have a capital and (if applicable) liquidity assessment every two years. 

- P2 firms have a capital and (if applicable) liquidity assessment every three to four years, by examining a firm’s own assessments of financial requirements. 

## Sources of Information on the FCA’s and PRA’s Supervisory Approach 

The FCA has been placing a greater emphasis on providing ‘informal guidance’ to firms and individuals, via speeches, public statements and Dear CEO letters, in addition to formal consultations. 

Therefore, firms and individuals within firms, must stay up to date with the methods of communication that the FCA is using to deliver its intentions and expectations of firms and/or individuals. 

Both the PRA and the FCA publish a number of documents which should be reviewed and assessed by firms. These documents include the following: 

- **Annual Report (FCA only)** 

   - This provides an update on what the FCA have delivered and achieved in the past year (period in question). 

- **Annual Business Plan (PRA & FCA)** 

   - For the FCA, this explains how they see their future role and priorities, how they intend to deliver them and how they measure their performance. 

   - For the PRA, the annual plan sets out the work plan for each of the PRA’s strategic goals to support delivery of their strategy. 

102 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

- 

## **Dear CEO Letters (FCA only)** 

   - An open letter sent to firms (providing specific services or performing specific activities) identifying concerns and issues that the FCA have identified, sometimes from thematic reviews of firm’s activities or issues, that they wish the industry to take note of. 

   - In some instances, the firm’s CEO would be required to provide an attestation to verify/confirm that they have undertaken an assessment relating to the concerns that the FCA identify in the letter. 

- 

## **Thematic Reviews (FCA only)** 

   - Used to assess a current or emerging risk regarding an issue or product across a number of firms in a market or sector. 

   - Also undertaken following the implementation of new rules, to assess that they are delivering the intended aims and objectives of the new rules. 

- 

## **Discussion Papers and Consultation Papers (PRA & FCA)** 

   - A publication that explores policy changes and/or an issue that needs addressing in the form of a consultation with the industry. 

   - The response period is open for a specific period of time. 

- 

## **Policy Statements (FCA & PRA)** 

   - This provides feedback on the consultation paper and approach adopted along with links to the final rules (statutory instrument). 

- 

## **Supervisory Statements (PRA only)** 

   - The supervisory statement provides feedback and approach adopted by the PRA, along with links to the final rules (statutory instrument). 

- 

## **Final Notices (FCA only)** 

   - A statutory notice issued when the FCA takes enforcement action against an individual and/or a regulated firm. 

- 

## **Enforcement Notices (FCA only)** 

   - Published by the FCA to advise individuals of a course of action undertaken by the FCA against an individual and/or a legal person (firm). 

   - All such documents are published by the FCA and PRA on their websites. These websites are a useful source of information and guidance for firms to assess how they might be affected and/or what might be relevant to them in terms of the regulated activities that they undertake. 

- 

## **Published Speeches and Websites (PRA & FCA)** 

   - Speeches made by PRA and/or FCA staff at events where they are invited to speak/discuss a specific topic (ie, benchmarks and market abuse) and/or their general approach to issues. 

- 

## **Enforcement Notices (FCA & PRA)** 

- Published by the PRA and the FCA to advise individuals of a course of action undertaken by them against an individual and/or a legal person (firm). 

- All such documents are published on their websites. 

- The websites are a useful source of information and guidance for firms to assess how they might be affected and/or what might be relevant to them in terms of the regulated activities that they undertake. 

103 

## **FCA Strategy 2025–30** 

In March 2025, the FCA published its ‘new’ five-year strategy, which focuses on: 

- deepening trust 

- rebalancing risk 

- supporting growth, and 

- improving lives. 

To achieve the strategy, the FCA will: 

- be a smarter regulator: 

   - efficient and effective regulator 

   - proportionate and predictable – easier to engage and deal with 

   - reform how they regulate 

- support growth: 

   - focus on the competitiveness of UK financial services 

- help consumers: 

   - product innovation and widened access 

   - consumers get the support and information that they need 

   - underpinning this will be the Consumer Duty 

- fight crime: 

   - work with firms that want to play their part in tackling crime (disrupt crime) 

   - consumers have the tools they need to protect themselves. 

The FCA highlighted five opportunities and challenges to meeting its five-year strategy, namely: 

- technology 

- growth 

- global uncertainty 

- challenging financial resilience, and 

- demographic change. 

Source of information: _FCA website_ 

## 2. Senior Managers & Certification Regime (SM&CR) 

Following the global financial bank crisis of 2007–08, the UK government created the Parliamentary Commission on Banking Standards (PCBS) to consider and report on professional standards and the culture of the UK banking sector. It took into account regulatory and competition investigations into the LIBOR rate-setting process and lessons learned about corporate governance, transparency, conflicts of interest, and their implications for regulation and for government policy. Initially it was called the Accountability Regime, but changed its name to the Senior Managers and Certification Regime (SM&CR). The essence of the ‘new’ regime was one of accountability for firms, as well as individuals who manage and direct the activities of the firm. 

104 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

The recommendations of the PCBS were accepted and adopted by the government, and inserted into the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013. 

The SM&CR came into effect for UK banks (deposit takers – credit institutions), branches of third country banks and insurance companies in 2016. It was phased in for other types of firms – notably Solvency II firms (including the Society of Lloyds, managing agents, incoming branches of non-UK firms and insurance special purpose vehicles), non-directive firms (insurers outside scope of Solvency II) and small run-off firms, FCA solo-regulated firms and claims management companies. 

## **All PRA regulated firms are now subject to the SM&CR, as well as the majority of FCA-regulated firms – except for appointed representatives and other limited types of firms – who are still subject to the Approved Persons Regime (APER).** 

There is no territorial limitation. The concept of an individual being captured by the Senior Managers Regime and/or the Certificate Regime is not decided on where the individual is based but rather the role that they perform in respect of the UK entity. 

## 2.1 Senior Managers Regime (SMR) 

The definition of a ‘senior manager’ is someone who is responsible for managing the firm on a dayto-day basis. Individuals are classed as an ‘SMF’ because they are undertaking a Senior Management Function. 

The regime is intended to capture the very senior individuals within banks who make the decisions and determine the direction of the firm in the discharge of regulated activities. 

This approach is slightly amended in respect of foreign branches, where the structure is different to UK banks. For foreign banks the regime will not catch overseas individuals who set the strategy or agree the strategy, rather it will capture those individuals who will implement that strategy in the UK branch. The regime only applies to ‘branch activity’. However, an individual based overseas could be captured if they are involved in implementation of the strategy and day-to-day management of the branch. 

The PRA and FCA have a list of key responsibilities that firms will be required to allocate among senior managers. 

Where an individual performing a ‘SMF’ is going to be absent from work for a period greater than 12 weeks, but is expected to return and their position is being left open by the firm, then the firm does not have to apply to remove their authorisation. 

It is only where the individual will not be returning to their role that requires authorisation (a ‘SMF’ role) that the firm must notify the PRA and/or the FCA. 

## 2.1.1 Application to Banks and Deposit Takers 

The PRA requires that all banks must have at least one individual performing as Chief Executive Officer (CEO), Chief Financial Officer (CFO) and Chairman SMF, as well as a head of key business area and group entity senior manager, and extends the Senior Managers Regime (SMR) to all board members not already caught by the PRA regime. 

105 

The FCA requires firms to have individuals in SMF roles of executive directors, significant responsibility senior manager, compliance oversight and money laundering reporting. 

Between the PRA and the FCA there are 17 senior management functions (five for the FCA and 12 for the PRA) that can/have to be allocated: these can be jointly allocated (although both would be responsible for the role/responsibility). 

For UK banks the FCA requires SMFs to be in the roles of executive directors, compliance oversight, **money laundering reporting officer (MLRO)** and other overall responsibility functions (previously called significant responsibility function). 

The ’other overall responsibility’ function is intended to cover individuals with overall responsibility for one or more key functions or identified risks, and who perform a function not caught by another SMF. This ‘overall function’ allows firms to designate senior executives who have ultimate responsibility for functions but would not otherwise be part of the SMF structure. 

Non-executive directors (NEDs) are also subject to the Senior Managers Regime – six functions are identified (five by the PRA and one by FCA), as noted below: 

1. Chairman. 

2. Chair of the Risk Committee. 

3. Chair of the Audit Committee. 

4. Chair of the Remuneration Committee. 

5. Senior Independent Director. 

6. Chair of the Nomination Committee (FCA only). 

Firms will have to undertake annual assessments to confirm that individuals performing a senior management function are fit and proper. 

Underneath the SMFs, the regulators have developed a list of key responsibilities (known as ‘prescribed responsibilities’) that firms will be required to allocate among senior managers – some are shared between the FCA and the PRA.

## 2.1.2 Application to Insurers/Insolvency II Firms 

The FCA approached identifying key management functions in insurers the same way as banks – the most senior in the firm, who control and direct the firm. 

There may be cases where a senior manager applies to perform both a PRA and FCA Senior Management Function at the same time. In this case, the FCA would apply an ‘Overlap Rule’. The Overlap Rule applies when a firm is seeking approval from the PRA for a candidate to perform a PRA-designated SMF and also intends this candidate to perform an FCA SMF, once the PRA gives its approval. The Overlap Rule applies to FCA governing functions only. 

The FCA decided against introducing requirements for firms to have a money laundering reporting officer (MLRO), rather firms only need to have an MLRO if the **Money Laundering Regulations (MLRs)** apply to them. However, they must still have appropriate systems and controls in place to prevent financial crime. 

Solvency II firms must ensure that one or more persons perform each of the following Senior Insurance Management Functions on its behalf: 

- Chief Executive Function. 

- Chief Finance Function. 

- Chairman. 

The FCA introduced the ‘Conduct Risk Oversight Officer’ function because of the size, scale and structurally-unique role of the Lloyd’s market. This function is consistent with the PRA’s current Underwriting Risk Oversight Function, which is also Lloyd’s-only. The Conduct Risk Oversight Officer for Lloyd’s is responsible for overseeing the conduct of business standards required by the Lloyd’s market for the managing agents who write business at Lloyd’s only. The senior manager is also accountable for monitoring and overseeing managing agents’ compliance with the Lloyd’s conduct of business standards. 

111 

The FCA applied the ‘Overall Responsibility’ requirement to all Solvency II firms (excluding small run-off firms and insurance special purpose vehicles (ISPVs)) and large non-directive firms (NDFs). Therefore, these firms must have a senior manager with overall responsibility for every activity, business area and management function in the firm. 

The FCA broadened the Compliance Oversight Function (SMF16) to cover all insurers for all requirements of the regulatory system for which the FCA is responsible. Therefore, insurers have to comply with all FCA rule requirements, as well as with directly applicable UK or onshored (retained) EU legislation where the FCA has responsibility for ensuring compliance. 

Individuals are allowed to cover for an approved person (SMF) without being approved, where the absence is temporary or cannot be reasonably foreseen, and the appointment is for less than 12 consecutive weeks. 

The FCA extended this 12-week rule to apply to responsibilities under the ‘Overall Responsibility’ requirement. 

SMFs functions 9 to 14 should be held by approved NEDs, rather than executives, The FCA adopted the same approach for NEDs as those that apply to banks  and deposit takers. 

The approach for non-executive directors (NEDs) is the same as for banks, as noted above. 

## PRA Senior Management Functions 

- SMF9 – Chair 

- SMF10 – Chair of Risk Committee. 

## FCA Functions 

- SMF11 – Chair of the Audit Committee 

- SMF12 – Chair of Remuneration Committee 

- SMF14 – Senior Independent Director. 

A combined List of PRA and FCA Senior Management Functions can be found on the FCA and PRA websites: 

- FCA Handbook – Supervision Sourcebook – Chapter 10C 

- PRA Handbook – Senior Management Functions. 

Details on the prescribed responsibilities for Insurers and Insolvency II firms can be found at: 

- FCA SYSC 24 (senior managers and certification regime: Allocation of prescribed responsibilities). _https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/SYSC/24/?view=chapter_ 

- PRA Allocation of responsibilities. 

_https://www.prarulebook.co.uk/pra-rules/allocation-of-responsibilities/_ 

112 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

## 2.1.3 Application to FCA Solo-Regulated Firms 

The SM&CR also applies to FCA firms in a different manner to how it applies to banks and deposit takers, due to the limited nature and impact that these firms could have on UK financial markets. 

It is a light touch, intended to be a more appropriate and proportionate approach by the FCA for these firms. The degree of application depends upon the ‘type’ of firm – as noted below: 

**==> picture [461 x 29] intentionally omitted <==**

**----- Start of picture text -----**<br>
Firm type Description<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


|**Firm type**|**Description**|
|---|---|
|||
|Limited Scope<br>Firm|Firms that have fewer requirements than core firms. This covers all firms that<br>would have had a limited application under the Approved Persons Regime<br>(APER), including:<br>•<br>limited permission consumer credit firms<br>•<br>all sole traders<br>•<br>authorised professional firms whose only regulated activities are non-<br>mainstream regulated activities<br>•<br>oil market participants<br>•<br>service companies<br>•<br>energy market participants<br>•<br>subsidiaries of local authorities or registered social landlords<br>•<br>insurance intermediaries whose principal business is not insurance<br>intermediation and who only have permission to carry on insurance<br>mediation activity in relation to non-investment insurance contracts<br>•<br>authorised internally managed Alternative Investment Funds (AIFs).|
|Core Firm|Firms that have a ‘baseline’ of SM&CR requirements applied.|
|Enhanced Firm|A small proportion of solo-regulated firms that will have to apply extra rules.|



## Core and Enhanced Firms 

These types of firms require the following SMFs: 

- SMF1 – Chief Executive. 

- SMF3 – Executive Director. 

- SMF9 – Chair. 

- SMF16 – Compliance Oversight. 

- SMF17 – Money Laundering Reporting Officer (MLRO). 

- SMF 18 – Other Overall responsibility (Enhanced Firms only). 

- SMF27 – Partner. 

113

Details on the prescribed responsibilities for FCA solo-regulated firms can be found at: 

- FCA – SYSC 24 (senior managers and certification regime: Allocation of prescribed responsibilities). _https://www.handbook.fca.org.uk/handbook/SYSC/24/?view=chapter_ 

## 2.1.4 Application to Benchmark Administrators 

The Senior Managers Regime has also been applied to benchmark administrators that do not undertake any other regulated activities. 

The FCA decided that benchmark administrators should automatically be subject to the core SMR, on the basis that they provide critical market infrastructure and the pricing of many financial instruments and contracts and the financial services sector depends on the accuracy and integrity of these benchmarks. 

Therefore, the FCA considers that these firms must have a healthy culture and high standards of personal conduct, avoiding and preventing harm to markets and consumers. 

The rules and requirements apply to UK-based benchmark administrators. 

Benchmark administrators that are classified as ‘core’ firms will be required to have/appoint individuals to the following SMFs: 

- SMF 1 – Chief Executive. 

- SMF 3 – Executive Director. 

114 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

- SMF 9 – Chair. 

- SMF 27 – Partner. 

Because these firms are subject to UK BMR (onshored/retained EU legislation), enacted via the statutory instrument titled ‘The Benchmarks (Amendment) (EU Exit) Regulations 2018’, they will not be required to appoint individuals to the function of Compliance Oversight (SMF16) and Money Laundering Reporting Officer (SMF17). 

Core benchmark administrators will be required to allocate three prescribed responsibilities: 

- (a) Performance by the firm of its obligations under the SMR, including implementation and oversight. 

- (b-1) Performance by the firm of its obligations in respect of notifications and training of the **Conduct Rules** . 

- (d) Responsibility for the firm’s policies and procedures for countering the risk that the firm might be used to further financial crime. 

By default, all benchmark administrators are classified as core firms for the purpose of the SM&CR, but for some firms the ‘Limited Scope Benchmarks Administrators’ will be more suitable – where they are required to appoint just one individual as a Senior Manager – the SMF 29 function (Limited Scope Function). These firms must apply to the FCA for a waiver to be classified as a Limited Scope Benchmark Administrator. 

The FCA did not apply the Certification Regime to benchmark administrators, where they do not undertake any other regulated activities. This is because the UK BMR places requirements on administrators to ensure employees are fit and proper – so this achieves the same result and outcome. 

The Conduct Rules apply to employees in benchmark administrators that undertake regulated and unregulated financial services activities. 

The FCA applied the Conduct Rules to firms subject to UK BMR for the regulated activity that they undertake. 

## 2.1.5 Application to All Types of Firms 

One of the most important elements of the regime is that each SMF is required to have a statement of responsibility and a responsibilities map (a responsibilities matrix) – a single document describing management and governance arrangements, which identify how responsibilities have been allocated to senior managers so that there are no gaps in accountability within the firm. For FCA solo-regulated firms, this applies to firms classified as ‘enhanced firms’. 

Firms must develop, and have in place, a responsibilities map, which clearly identifies the structure and individuals who are senior managers. These must be allocated to an SMF; sharing of responsibilities is permitted between individuals – although the PRA and the FCA see this being used in very limited circumstances. In these circumstances, both individuals would share the responsibility – which must be noted on the statement of responsibility. 

Firms must explain in their responsibility map why a responsibility is allocated jointly. 

115 

## 2.1.6 Statements of Responsibilities 

As part of the regulatory application process, firms must provide statements of responsibilities to the FCA/PRA for approval. These must be shown to the regulators as part of the application process – they MUST be approved by the regulators. The maps identify which parts of the firm’s business/activities senior managers are personally responsible for – these will then be used by the regulators when there has been a breach of rules/regulations. These statements must be accepted by the individual – they will be required to sign them, taking ownership of them, including maintaining and ensuring that they are up to date). 

## 2.1.7 Handover Material 

Where a person is becoming an SMF or is an existing SMF but is taking on new responsibilities, there is a requirement that necessary information and material reasonably expected for them to perform their responsibilities effectively, is made available in accordance with relevant regulatory requirements. 

Firms must have a policy about how they comply with this requirement. These handover documents must be practical and helpful, including an assessment of what issues should be prioritised, containing judgment and opinion, not just facts and figures. 

## 2.1.8 Duty of Responsibility 

In the original PCBS recommendations and the subsequent FCA and PRA consultations, upon the occurrence of a regulatory breach, the senior manager would be deemed to have breached their personal duties and be guilty of misconduct. The onus would have been on the senior manager to satisfy the regulators that they had taken ‘reasonable steps’ to prevent the breach from occurring. The FCA would have used the statement of responsibilities and responsibilities map to identify the relevant individual with accountability and responsibility. 

However, HM Treasury changed their approach, and in the Bank of England and **Financial Services Act 2016** , the burden of proof was shifted back to the regulators, with the PRA and FCA now being required to demonstrate that the senior manager (SMF) did not take such steps as a person in their position could reasonably be expected to have taken to avoid the firm’s contravention occurring. 

Therefore, the regulators have a burden of proof hurdle before they can bring disciplinary proceedings against the senior manager (SMF). 

This is called ‘the duty of responsibility’. 

## 2.1.9 Overall and Local Responsibility 

One of the main purposes and aims behind the SM&CR was that one or more individuals from a firm have responsibility for each of the activities, business areas and management functions of their firm. 

Both the FCA and the PRA expect that individuals to whom overall responsibility for a function has been allocated are the most senior individuals within the firm for managing and/or supervising that function. 

116 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

They will be either a member of the governing body (management body or equivalent) or will report directly to the governing body (management body or equivalent). 

The individual(s) must be sufficiently senior and credible and have sufficient resources and authority. The FCA is aware that smaller firms may allocate responsibility to a small group of individuals – but for all firms, the FCA expects that when allocated to individuals then those individuals should be able to perform their responsibilities effectively. 

## 2.1.10 ‘Long-Term Absence’ – Impacts to Senior Managers (SMFs) 

In 2021 the FCA and the PRA published a clarification on their expectation for firms to notify them when an individual who is in a SMF role, takes temporary leave for more than 12 weeks. 

In cases where an individual who is in a SMF position is temporarily absent from their role, and their role has been kept open/they will be returning to their role, the firm will not need to notify the FCA (and, where applicable, the PRA) that the individual’s approval should be removed. 

Firms need to ensure that for this role they obtain approval for another individual to perform that required function during the interim period. 

This does not change the notification requirements for firms to notify the FCA when the individual will not be returning to their role. 

## 2.1.11 Consumer Duty – Implications for Senior Managers 

(Refer to section 6 of this chapter for further details on the Consumer Duty requirements.) 

Following the application of the Consumer Duty, senior managers are tasked with the following where the Consumer Duty applies (ie, the firm is considered in scope of the Consumer Duty due them having a relationship with retail clients and/or the products or services that they provide can have an impact on the outcomes of retail clients): 

- The Consumer Duty is reflected in firms’ strategies, governance, leadership and people policies. 

- Senior managers ensure that their firm has appropriate oversight of customer outcomes through their systems and controls: 

   - senior managers will be held accountable for delivering ‘good consumer outcomes’ within their area of responsibility within the firm. 

- Firms must appoint a Consumer Duty champion at board level (or equivalent governing body), where they, the CEO and the Chairman of the Board ensure that Consumer Duty is regulatory discussed and raised. 

## 2.2 Certification Regime 

Individuals subject to the Certification Regime are not approved by the regulators; rather firms make an assessment that they are deemed ‘fit and proper’ to perform their role and provide them with an annual ‘certificate’ that they can perform their role. 

117 

There are different approaches taken by the PRA and the FCA due to their statutory objectives. The PRA approach limits the scope to ‘material risk takers’ (MRTs) as defined in the Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR) (ie, individuals who are subject to the Remuneration Code – Code staff) while the FCA approach includes individuals performing their function from within the UK or dealing with a client in the UK who: 

- would previously have been performing a **significant influence function (SIF)** role under APER, but who now are outside the SMR – as they are not an SMF (eg, CASS oversight, benchmark submitter, or administrators, proprietary traders and previous CF29 significant manager function-holders) 

- are performing a significant harm function, including engaged in wholesale activities on the basis of dealing as principle or agent (traders, including Algorithm traders) and bring about deals in investments (sales staff) 

- are in customer-facing roles which are subject to qualification requirements 

- supervise or manage a Certified Person, where they are not an SMF 

- the individual is employed by a UK bank, and/or the UK branch of a third country bank 

- for third country banks, the individual is carrying out ‘branch activity’ as defined by the FCA. 

Therefore, the PRA and the FCA require the responsibility for assessing the ‘fitness and propriety’ of staff who perform and/or carry out ‘significant harm’ functions to firms. 

Significant-harm functions include: 

- CASS oversight 

- benchmark submission and administration 

- proprietary trader 

- significant management 

- functions requiring qualifications 

- managers of certification employees 

- material risk takers. 

The term ‘material risk taker’ is defined in the PRA Remuneration Rulebook as an individual whose activities, carried out on behalf of the firm, could have a material impact on the firm’s risk profile. 

Firms must provide a ‘certificate’ to individuals annually which states that they (the firm) are satisfied that the individual is ‘fit and proper’ to perform the function, it will also set out their role and responsibilities. 

Where individuals are performing multiple certification functions – fitness and propriety must be assessed for each certification function, however a single certificate can cover the multiple functions being performed. 

Where no certificate is issued to an individual, because they are deemed not to meet the ‘fitness and propriety’ text, then the firm must give written notice explaining what steps (if any) they propose to take, as well as notifying the FCA of the circumstances. 

The Certification Regime could impact overseas employees of UK firms. Firstly, to individuals deemed to be ‘material risk takers’ (or ‘Code’ staff) for the firm due to their role, no matter where they are located, and secondly, where an individual overseas is dealing with, or has contact with, UK clients. Where an individual is based overseas and does not deal with UK clients and is performing another of the Certification functions (other than material risk takers) then they will not be a certified person. 

118 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

The term ‘code staff’ refers to employees whose activities have a material impact on the firm’s risk profile. 

## 2.3 Conduct Rules 

The FCA approach to the Conduct Rules means that all UK employees (with the exception of staff carrying out purely ancillary functions, such as cleaners and catering staff) will be within the regulatory scope. 

The PRA, on the other hand, applied the Conduct Rules to senior managers and individuals who fall within the PRA Certification regime (material risk-takers). 

By applying the Conduct Rules to all levels within a firm, the FCA is trying to embed a grass roots understanding of what acceptable and unacceptable behaviour is. Conduct Rules appear to be the FCA’s key tool in reinforcing the importance of appropriate values and behaviours at all levels within firms. 

Therefore, firms are obliged to make all staff aware of their obligations and provide training on how the rules apply. Firms must notify the regulators when there has been a breach of the Conduct Rules. 

The FCA applied the six individual Conduct Rules to standard NEDs and the additional ‘Senior Manager’ Conduct Rule. 

## First Tier – Individual Conduct Rules – PRA and FCA 

Rule 1 You must act with integrity. Rule 2 You must act with due skill, care and diligence. Rule 3 You must be open and cooperative with the FCA, the PRA and other regulators. 

## Individual Conduct Rules – FCA only 

Rule 4 You must pay due regard to the interests of customers and treat them fairly. Rule 5 You must observe proper standards of market conduct. Rule 6 You must act to deliver good outcomes for retail customers. 

Individual Conduct Rule 6 will not apply to individuals and/or firms that are not subject to Consumer Duty, meaning that Individual Conduct Rule 4 will continue to apply. 

## Second Tier – Senior Manager Conduct Rules – PRA and FCA 

SC1 You must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which you are responsible is controlled effectively. 

- SC2 You must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which you are responsible complies with relevant requirements and standards of the regulatory system. 

- SC3 You must take reasonable steps to ensure that any delegation of your responsibilities is to an appropriate person and that you oversee the discharge of the delegated responsibility effectively. 

- SC4 You must disclose appropriately any information of which the FCA or the PRA would reasonably expect notice. 

119 

## 2.4 Regulatory References 

A firm subject to the SM&CR must obtain a regulatory reference before it appoints an individual to a senior management function (SMF) or to perform a role that would be subject to the Certification Regime. 

Where a firm receives a request to provide a regulatory reference, due to an ex-employee moving to a new firm, they are obliged to provide such reference and confirm if they consider the individual to be ‘fit and proper’. They are only required to disclose instances of conduct that occurred or existed for six years from the date of the reference request being received. 

The FCA developed a template for firms to use when requesting a regulatory reference, which can be found in SYSC 22, Annex 1 (Template for regulatory references given by SM&CR firms and disclosure requirements)] 

## 2.5 The Directory 

The FCA Financial Services Register includes the ‘Directory’, providing a single central location for information on individuals subject to either the APER or the SM&CR. The register is updated by the FCA and/or the PRA for senior managers, whereas individuals subject to the Certification Regime would be managed by the firms themselves. 

One of the objectives of the register is to reduce potential harm to consumers, strengthen market integrity and enhance competition. 

The register makes: 

- information public on additional individuals carrying out a wider range of roles (including those not approved such as financial advisers, traders, portfolio managers and additional directors) 

- information public on the individuals and the senior managers the FCA continues to approve in a way that is more accessible and user friendly, and 

- enables users to find information on these individuals in a single public location. 

The information is available in an aggregated form that can be extracted free of charge. Customers, firms and other stakeholders are able to use the Directory to easily find and check the identity of relevant individuals. 

The register allows customers to benefit from greater information to make informed decisions on who to do business with, using the following features: 

- **Workplace location** – list of individuals active in specific areas. 

- **Business qualified to carry on** – list of individuals who hold appropriate qualifications, for example, to provide mortgage advice. 

- **Regulatory sanctions and prohibitions** – the facility to check whether individuals are prohibited by the FCA. 

_Source of information: https://register.fca.org.uk/s/_ 

120 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

## 2.6 Review of the SM&CR 

In 2020, the PRA published a report on its first review of the implementation and application of the SM&CR to PRA firms (ie, deposit taking banks and certain insurance companies) in the period 2019 to 2020. 

The PRA identified three ‘key’ themes and follow up actions and recommendations for each of them: 

- **Theme 1 – Holding individuals to account through the SM&CR** 

   - **Conduct and regulatory references** – ensure regulatory references are used in an appropriate manner. 

   - **Remuneration** – articulate the link between the SM&CR and remuneration adjustments. 

   - **Senior manager expectations** – underline the responsibility of prescribed responsibilities allocated to SMFS and Certified individuals. 

- **Theme 2 – Myth busting and clarifying expectations** 

   - **Diversity** – reaffirm the appetite for diverse skills and experience among senior management. 

   - **Collective accountability** – further analysis on board responsibilities and individual’s accountability to see if they are mutually reinforcing. 

   - **Interim appointments** – clarify (along with the FCA) expectations where a senior manager takes temporary leave for longer than 12 weeks. 

- **Theme 3 – Application of the SM&CR to different business models** 

   - **Allocation of responsibilities** – use of ‘Head of Key Business Area’; the way in which designation of certain individuals works in conjunction with the SM&CR; further guidance on allocating prescribed responsibilities and the potential for smaller firms to submit SM&CR documentation less frequently. 

## 2.7 HM Treasury Paper – Proposals for a ‘SM&CR’ for Financial Market Infrastructures 

In 2021, HM Treasury published a consultation paper setting out its intentions to create, and apply, a SM&CR regime for Financial Market Infrastructures (FMI) which they define as Central Clearing Counterparties, Central Securities Depositories and Payment systems (recognised under the Banking Act 2009). 

The key features of the proposed SM&CR for FMIs are intended to be similar to the existing SM&CR regime, as set out in Part 5 of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (‘FSMA’), but modified to recognise the fact that FMIs are not authorised persons within the FSMA meaning of the term. 

The Bank of England (BoE) would be granted new powers to implement, supervise and enforce the following: 

- A **Senior Managers Regime** – this would give the BoE the power to determine whether individuals who perform roles that pose a potential risk to financial stability or to the continuing functioning of the FMI have the appropriate competence, expertise and probity to carry out their roles. 

- A **Certification Regime** – this would require firms to certify any individual who performs a ‘specified function’ that could cause significant harm to the FMI or its users as fit and proper, both at recruitment and annually thereafter. 

121 

- **Conduct Rules** for all employees, which set minimum, high-level requirements regarding the conduct of individuals where necessary or expedient for advancing the BoE’s financial stability objective. 

The proposed regime would strengthen the individual accountability of senior managers within FMIs and would provide an effective and proportionate means of ensuring high standards of conduct among all staff. This would ensure that the firms which underpin the proper functioning and overall stability of the UK’s financial system are subject to the highest regulatory standards. 

In June 2022, HM Treasury published an update on responses received to the consultation and the next steps. HM Treasury noted that they will proceed with an SM&CR for CCPs and CSDs with particular details to be set out in secondary legislation and the FCA rules. 

The government intends to have the option to extend the SM&CR to certain other systemic financial services entities in the future (credit rating agencies and recognised investment exchanges) should it decide that it is appropriate to apply the SM&CR to them. Therefore, they will legislate to create a new SM&CR ‘gateway’, when parliamentary time allows, which will enable HM Treasury to lay statutory instruments to apply the SM&CR to CCPs, CSDs, and, in the future, potentially to other financial services entities. 

The government also intends to legislate to implement the SM&CR for recognised payment systems and specified service providers. However, they noted that will be taken forward separately to the creation of the ‘gateway’ and will have a longer timeframe. 

Please refer to chapter 5, section 3 for details on the SM&CR review, which was contained within the Edinburgh Reforms announced in December 2022. 

## 3. Approved Persons Regime (APER) 

The Approved Persons Regime (APER) only applies to a limited number of regulated firms, which mainly includes appointed representatives. 

The Approved Person Regime no longer applies to PRA regulated firms – all PRA regulated firms are now subject to the SM&CR. 

FSMA refers to ‘authorised persons’ and ‘approved persons’. For authorisation purposes, the term ‘person’ refers to legal persons rather than individual human beings. A limited company, for example, is a legal person. 

- Authorised persons, generally, authorisation, as discussed in chapter 1, section 1.3, is required under s19 of FSMA for firms or businesses that are carrying out regulated activities by way of business in the UK. In order to obtain authorisation, firms must satisfy the threshold conditions (see section 8). 

- Approved persons, under s59, individual people who work for such authorised persons (ie, authorised firms) as employees or appointed representatives must be approved if carrying out what are referred to as controlled functions. 

122 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

An individual is only permitted to perform a controlled function after they have been granted approved person status by the FCA. 

The Financial Services Act 2012 (‘the Act’) amended the powers to regulate approved persons and set out how the powers may be exercised by the PRA and FCA. The main relevant aspect of change was the extension of the Statements of Principle in APER to a wider set of activities, and their application to people approved by either regulator, meaning that both regulators will have the ability to discipline certain categories of approved person. 

The FCA may specify customer dealing functions for any type of firm, and only the FCA may specify SIFs (significant influence functions) for solo-regulated firms. 

The Act also includes the following detailed provisions on approved persons: 

- The FCA can specify functions that will involve the person performing them dealing with customers of a regulated firm, or the property of those customers. 

- The statements of principle (contained in APER) can relate to the conduct expected of persons not just in relation to the controlled functions they perform, but also in relation to other functions they perform, where those functions relate to the firm for which they hold their approval for carrying on regulated activities. 

Individuals carrying out SIFs tend, by the nature of their roles, to occupy relatively senior positions within firms. In many cases, particularly those of the most senior roles, such as the chief executive, this means they will inevitably have responsibilities for both conduct and prudential issues. Therefore, the Act takes account of this. 

## 3.1 The FCA’s Approach to Approved Persons and Specifying Controlled Functions 

## Controlled Functions 

The controlled functions are: 

- **Governing function** – individuals responsible for directing the affairs of the business. If the business is a company, then they will be the directors of that company. If the business is a partnership, then they will be the partners. 

   - The ‘governing function’ is a significant influence function (SIF), on the basis that persons fulfilling these roles exercise a significant influence over the conduct of a firm’s affairs. 

- **Customer function** – individuals who would be giving/providing advice on dealing, arranging deals and managing investments. The individuals will have contact with customers in fulfilling their role, examples of customer functions are an investment adviser, the customer trading function or the investment management function. Customer functions are not SIFs. 

123 

The following table sets out the FCA Controlled Functions. 

**==> picture [461 x 186] intentionally omitted <==**

**----- Start of picture text -----**<br>
Type CF Description of Controlled Function<br>FCA-governing functions* 1 Director function<br>2 Non-executive director function<br>3 Chief executive function<br>4 Partner function<br>5 Director of unincorporated association function<br>Customer-dealing function 30 Customer function<br>* FCA-significant influence functions<br>**----- End of picture text -----**<br>


## Governing Function 

The FCA Handbook states that someone who is approved for a governing function, including the chief executive function, does not need separate approval for that part of the customer function (CF30) which relates to bidding in emissions auctions. 

Details on the Statements of Principle and Code of Practice are in the APER Statement of Principle and Code of Practice for Approved Person sourcebook, located in the ‘High Level Standards’ section of the FCA Handbook. 

## 4. COCON (Conduct Rules) 

The PCBS recommended that the regulators develop a new set of banking standard rules that drew the existing statements of principles in APER (Statements of Principle and Code of Practice for Approved Persons) but apply to a wider group of individuals. The FCA approach means that the Conduct Rules apply to all UK employees of relevant firms, except staff carrying out purely ancillary functions who perform a role that is not specific to the financial services business of the firm, while the PRA applies the Conduct Rules to individuals performing a Senior Management Function and individuals who fall within the PRA Certification Regime. 

By extending the Conduct Rules to all levels within a firm, the FCA is trying to embed a grass roots understanding of what acceptable and unacceptable behaviour is. 

Conduct Rules appear to be the FCA’s key tool in reinforcing the importance of appropriate values and behaviours at the most junior levels of firms. 

Refer to section 2.3 of this chapter for further details. 

124 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

In 2023, the PRA levied a fine on an individual for a breach of the Senior Manager Conduct Rule 3 ( _‘You must take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the firm for which you are responsible complies with relevant requirements and standards of the regulatory system’_ ). 

The PRA fined the ex-CIO (Chief Information Officer) at TSB Bank plc, which was migrating its IT systems from a legacy system to a new platform. The failure led to customers not being able to access their accounts, payments from their accounts were not made (including payment for mortgages and loans), and it became headline news for the wrong reasons. 

In 2022, TSB Bank Plc was fined jointly by the PRA (£18,900,000) and the FCA (£29,750,000), with the individual being fined £81,620 in April 2023 by the PRA. 

The individual was responsible for providing leadership and strategic direction to the IT function, ensuring that the IT function was suitably experienced and qualified to carry out its responsibilities, as well as identifying, assessing, managing and reporting risks associated with the running of the IT function in line with the banks risk appetite. The CIO was also responsible for the performance of its obligations under the PRA’s Outsourcing Rules, and this responsibility was split with another senior manager. Under that split, the individual was responsible for the bank’s key outsourcing relationships and accountable for the operational relationship with third parties in relation to IT. 

The PRA noted that it expected the CIO to act reasonably in carrying out their role and responsibilities, particularly in relation to the identification and mitigation of risks from an IT perspective. Under the Senior Manager Conduct Rules, the PRA also required the individual to take reasonable steps to ensure that the business of the bank for which they were responsible complied with relevant regulatory requirements and standards. 

The PRA expects persons performing a SMF to take reasonable steps to ensure that the business has operating procedures and systems which include well-defined steps for such compliance and for ensuring that the business is run prudently. This required the individual to take reasonable steps in relation to the identification and mitigation of risks relating to the readiness of the bank’s contractors and subcontractors. 

The PRA identified that the ex-CIO had breached Senior Manager Conduct Rule 2 as they had failed to take reasonable steps to ensure that the bank complied with the PRA’s Outsourcing Rules in adequately managing and appropriately supervising its outsourcing arrangement. In particular, the CIO failed to obtain sufficient assurance from the outsource provider in relation to the readiness of the new platform. 

## 5. High-Level Standards: Introduction 

The high-level standards section of the FCA Handbook refers to the overarching standards applying to all authorised firms and approved persons. It contains the following source books: 

- **PRIN** – Principles for Businesses – the fundamental obligations of all firms. 

- **SYSC** – Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls – the responsibilities of directors and senior management. 

- **COCON** – Code of Conduct. 

125 

- **COND** – Threshold Conditions – minimum standards for authorisation. 

- **APER** – Statements of Principle and Code of Practice for Approved Persons – the fundamental obligations of approved persons. 

- **FIT** – the Fit and Proper Test for employees and Senior Personnel. 

- **FINMAR** – Financial Stability and Market Confidence. 

- **TC** – **Training and Competence** – requirements for staff. 

- **GEN** – General Provisions. 

- **FEES** – Fee provisions for funding the regulatory bodies. 

The following seven sections cover the most important areas of these high–level standards, other than ‘COCON’ (see section 4) and ‘APER’ (see section 3). 

## 6. Principles for Businesses (PRIN) 

The FCA Handbook includes 12 key Principles for Businesses, which authorised firms must observe. The principles apply with respect to the carrying out of regulated activities, activities that include dealing in investments as principal, ancillary activities in relation to designated investment business, home finance activity, insurance mediation activity, accepting deposits, and the communication and approval of financial promotions. 

If a firm breaches any of the principles that apply to it, it will be liable to disciplinary sanctions. However, the FCA and the PRA must be able to prove that the firm has been at fault.
