- pension products − having the primary purpose of providing the investor with an income in retirement and which entitle the investor to certain benefits (ie, occupational pension schemes – where a contribution from the employer is required by national law and where the employer or the employee has no choice as to the pension product or provider) 

- annuities where the amount payable to the annuitant does not fluctuate (eg, a purchased life annuity that pays a fixed amount of income for life or an annuity that pays a fixed income for a specified term), and 

- certain securities such as issued by member states, their regional or local authorities, central banks, public international bodies, non-profit making bodies or credit institutions. 

## Key Information Documents (KIDs) 

The PRIIPs Regulation requires that a KID is a stand-alone, standardised document prepared for each investment. A KID can be up to a maximum of three A4 pages. 

240 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

Each KID will need to contain the following information, presented in a pre-determined sequence of sections. The sections are as follows: 

- Purpose. 

- What is this product? 

- What are the risks and what could I get in return? 

- What happens if (name of the PRIIP manufacturer) is unable to pay out? 

- What are the costs? 

- How long should I hold it and can I take money out early? 

- How can I complain? 

- Other relevant information. 

## 14.10.6 Changes to PRIIPS Scope and Amendments to Regulatory Technical Standards 

In 2021, the FCA consulted on proposals to address identified persistent concerns of the onshored (retained EU) PRIIPS Regulation. 

The Policy Statement and final rules introduced the following changes: 

- Clarify the scope of the PRIIPs Regulation for corporate bonds: 

   - making it clearer that certain common features of these instruments do not make them into a PRIIP. 

- Introduce interpretative guidance to clarify what it means for a PRIIP to be ‘made available’ to retail investors. 

- Amend the PRIIPs RTS to: 

   - replace the requirements and methodologies for presentation of performance scenarios in the KID with a requirement for narrative information on performance to be provided 

   - address the potential for some PRIIPs to be assigned an inappropriately low summary risk indicator in the KID 

   - address concerns about certain applications of the ‘slippage’ methodology when calculating transaction costs. 

The changes took effect in 2022. 

Refer to chapter 5, section 4, for details on the UK ‘new’ retail disclosure framework. 

On 19 September 2024, the Government and the FCA issued a joint statement regarding the reforms to retail disclosure requirements. The Government will introduce legislation that will exempt investment trusts from the current PRIIPS regulation. This is a temporary measure, as they will be included within the scope of the future UK retail focused disclosure framework. 

The FCA issued a forbearance statement to provide clarity and certainty for firms ahead any the new legislation taking effect. Therefore, from 19 September 2024 and until the new UK retail disclosure framework comes into effect (date to be confirmed), they will not take any supervisory or enforcement action, if an investment trust chooses not to comply with the requirements of the current PRIIPS regulations and associated technical standards. 

241 

## 14.11 Product Governance (PROD) 

## 14.11.1 Product Governance Requirements 

The purpose of the MiFID II requirements, adopted into the FCA’s Product Intervention and Product Governance (PROD) Sourcebook, is to ensure that firms have adequate and appropriate product oversight and governance processes in place. 

Product oversight and governance refers to the ‘systems and controls’ that firms must have in place to design, approve, market and manage products throughout the products’ life cycle to ensure they meet legal and regulatory requirements. 

Good governance results in products that meet the needs of one or more identifiable target markets, are sold to clients in the target market assessment audience by appropriate distribution channels and deliver appropriate outcomes for clients. The MiFID rules and requirements largely replicated the previous UK regime applicable to retail structured products. 

However, the legislation did not tailor the new requirements to financial instruments; the result is that they apply to vastly different financial instruments in terms of risk profile and complexity. Determining the proportional application was one of the key challenges that firms faced in implementing MiFID. 

The scope of what constitutes a manufacturer and a distributor is purposely wide. A manufacturer creates, develops, issues and/or designs MiFID financial instruments for sale to clients. This does include firms advising corporate issuers on the launch of new products. 

Manufacturers must implement a thorough product approval process consisting of: 

- identifying the target market for each product (target market analysis) 

- conducting a scenario analysis regarding risk (scenario analysis) 

- providing distributors with the appropriate information about the product 

- implementing management body oversight of the product governance framework, including managing/identifying any possible conflicts of interest and determining whether the product could represent a threat to the orderly functioning of the market 

- establishing a product review process which includes gathering information from distributors to determine if the product remains consistent with the identified target market, and 

- reviewing the charging structure. 

A distributor offers, recommends or sells an investment product and/or service to a client. 

Distributors must ensure that their products meet the needs of the relevant client market by: 

- at a business level – conducting target market analysis of their distribution strategy 

- ensuring the products they distribute are appropriate for the target market of their client base 

- carrying out periodic reviews of their distribution and providing management information to manufacturers, and 

- conducting a scenario analysis regarding risk (scenario analysis). 

242 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

Following the introduction of the Consumer Duty, there are new requirements that apply to firms regarding product development. 

Firms would only need to comply with one set of relevant rules, with the requirements in the Consumer Duty or the requirements in PROD (refer to section 6 of this chapter for details on the Consumer Duty). 

## 14.11.2 Target Market Assessment 

## Manufacturers 

Manufacturers are required to identify the potential target market for each financial instrument at a sufficiently granular level and: 

- specify the type(s) of client for whose needs and objectives the financial instrument is compatible, and 

- identify any group(s) of client(s) for whom the needs and objectives of the financial instrument are not compatible. 

Manufacturers must determine for each financial instrument whether it meets the identified needs and objectives of the target market, including undertaking an assessment of the following: 

- whether the financial instrument’s profile is consistent with the target market, and 

- whether the design of the financial instrument is driven by features that benefit the client and not the firm’s business model (which relies on poor outcomes to be profitable). 

Manufacturers that distribute their products through other firms must determine the needs and objectives of the clients for whom the product is compatible based on: 

- their theoretical knowledge of, and past experience with, the financial instrument or similar financial instruments, and 

- the needs and objectives of potential end clients. 

## Distributors 

Distributors must determine the ‘target market’ for the respective financial instrument, even if the target market was not defined by the manufacturer. 

When identifying the target market for a financial instrument, distributors must consider the following categories: 

1. client type 

2. knowledge and experience (specific knowledge of product type and product features; length and depth of experience in product type and product features) 

3. financial situation, with a focus on the ability to bear losses (specify the percentage of losses target clients should be able and willing to afford): 

   - to a level specified in the product 

   - up to the amount invested, or 

   - beyond the invested amount. 

243 

4. risk tolerance and compatibility of the risk/reward profile (general attitude clients have to the risks of investments; risk attitudes should be categorised, ie, ‘risk-oriented’, ‘speculative’, ‘balanced’, ‘conservative’), and 

5. client’s objectives and needs (specify the investment objectives and needs of target clients that a product is designed to meet). 

In addition, distributors must obtain information from manufacturers in respect of the product(s). 

## 14.11.3 Scenario Analysis 

Manufacturers must undertake a scenario analysis to assess the risks of poor outcomes for clients and to determine in which circumstances these poor outcomes may occur. 

The scenario analysis must cover negative market conditions, under the ‘what would happen if’ scenario: 

- The market environment deteriorates. 

- The manufacturer experiences financial difficulties. 

- The financial instrument fails to be commercially viable. 

- Demand for the financial instrument is much higher than expected. 

Manufacturers must consider the charging structure proposed for each financial instrument, including assessment of the following: 

- whether the financial instrument’s costs and charges are compatible with the needs, objectives and characteristics of the target market 

- whether the charges undermine the financial instrument’s return expectations, such as where the costs or charges equal, exceed or remove almost all the expected tax advantages linked to a financial instrument 

- whether the charging structure of the financial instrument is appropriately transparent for the target market, such that it does not disguise charges or is too complex to understand. 

Manufacturers must consider whether the financial instrument may represent a threat to the orderly functioning, or to the stability, of financial markets before deciding to proceed with the launch of the financial instrument. 

## 14.11.4 Information Sharing and Disclosure 

A manufacturer must make available to any distributor of that financial instrument: 

- all appropriate information on the financial instrument 

- all appropriate information on the product approval process 

- the identified target market of the financial instrument, including information about the target market assessment undertaken 

- information about the appropriate channels for distribution of the financial instrument, and 

- must ensure that the information is of an adequate standard to enable distributors to understand and recommend or sell the financial instrument properly. 

244 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

When providing information to distributors, a manufacturer should make it clear if that information is not intended for end client use. 

To support the reviews carried out by manufacturers, a distributor must provide the following information on each financial instrument it distributes: 

- sales information, and 

- post-sale review undertaken. 

## 14.11.5 Proportionality 

Manufacturers must regularly review the product or financial instruments it manufactures, taking into account any event that could materially affect the potential risk to the identified target market. 

In doing so, a manufacturer must assess for each financial instrument at least the following: 

- whether the financial instrument remains consistent with the needs, characteristics and objectives of the identified target market 

- whether the intended distribution strategy remains appropriate 

- whether the financial instrument is being distributed to the target market, and 

- whether the financial instrument is reaching clients for whose needs, characteristics and objectives the financial instrument is not compatible. 

In addition, it would need to review financial instruments prior to any further issue or relaunch if it is aware of any event that could materially affect the potential risk to clients and identify crucial events that would affect the potential risk or return expectations of the financial instrument. 

Distributors must regularly review the financial instruments they distribute and the investment services they provide, taking into account any event that could materially affect the potential risk to the identified target market. This would encompass assessing whether the financial instrument remains consistent with the needs and objectives of the identified target market and whether the distribution strategy remains appropriate. 

## 14.12 Dealing and Managing (COBS 11) 

## 14.12.1 Best Execution 

The rules on best execution apply to UK MiFID and non-UK MiFID firms and business. However, there is an exemption from the requirements for firms acting in the capacity of an operator of a regulated CIS when purchasing or selling units or shares in that scheme. 

The best execution rules under COBS require firms to execute client orders on the terms that are most favourable to their client. 

245 

Under the original MiFID rules, investment firms were required to take ‘reasonable steps’ to obtain the best possible outcome for their clients. MiFID II intensified the best execution obligation by replacing the term ‘reasonable steps’ with ‘all sufficient steps’. Broadly, they apply if a firm owes a contractual or agency obligation to its client, ie, it is acting on behalf of that client – firms must obtain the best possible outcomes for their client. 

For each transaction it engages in, an investment firm must assess (this is called the four-fold test) if the client is placing a legitimate reliance on the firm and/or the firm is using its discretion. In relation to over-the-counter (OTC) transactions, when a duty of best execution exists, investment firms must have processes in place to consistently check the fairness of the pricing they provide and retain any relevant market data to allow them to justify their pricing decisions. 

The ‘four-fold’ test, noted below, identifies the level to which the client places reliance on the firm to achieve best execution. Firms must assess each of the tests individually and determine whether they owe the client a duty to ensure ‘best execution’. 

- Did the client or the investment firm initiate the transaction? 

- Is the client able to ‘shop around for prices/quotes, or are they only able to ask you for a quote? 

- Will the client be unable to identify the relative levels of price transparency within a market? 

- Has the client confirmed that they place reliance with the investment firm? 

Given the differences in the structures of financial instruments, it may be difficult to identify and apply a uniform standard of, and procedure for, best execution that will be effective and appropriate for all classes of instruments. Therefore, best execution obligations should be applied in a manner that takes into account all the different circumstances associated with the particular types of financial instruments. 

Firms must take all sufficient steps to obtain, when executing orders, the best possible result for their clients, taking into account the execution factors. These factors are price, costs, speed, likelihood of execution and settlement, size, nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of an order. The relative importance of each factor will depend on the following criteria and characteristics: 

- the client, including how they are categorised 

- the client order 

- the financial instruments involved, and 

- the execution venues to which that order could be directed. 

Best execution is not merely how to achieve the best price. Any of the other factors mentioned above must also be considered and, depending on the criteria or characteristics, could be given precedence. For some transactions, for example, the likelihood of execution should be given precedence over the speed of execution. In other transactions, the direct and/or implicit execution costs of a particular venue could be so high as to be given precedence over the price of the instrument on this venue. 

## **The Role of Price** 

For retail clients, firms must take account of the total consideration for the transaction, ie, the price of the financial instrument and the costs relating to execution, including all expenses directly related to it, such as execution venue fees, clearing and settlement fees, and any fees paid to third parties. 

246 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

## **Best Execution (Venues)** 

When a firm could execute the client’s order on more than one execution venue, the firm must take into account both its own costs and the costs of the relevant venues in assessing which will give the best outcome. Its own commissions should not allow it to discriminate unfairly between execution venues, and a firm should not charge a different commission or spread to clients for execution in different venues, if that difference does not reflect actual differences in the cost to the firm of executing on those venues. 

## 14.12.2 Order Execution Policies 

Firms are required to monitor not only the execution quality obtained but also the quality and appropriateness of their execution arrangements and policies. Ongoing execution monitoring must be reviewed by senior management and/or relevant committees and fed back into execution policies and arrangements to drive improvements in the firm’s processes. 

Firms must establish an order execution policy to enable them to obtain the best possible results for their clients. For each class of financial instrument the firm deals in, this must include information about the different execution venues where the firm executes its client orders, and the factors that will affect the choice of venue used. Furthermore, the policy must include those venues that enable the firm consistently to obtain the best possible result for its clients. 

Firms are not expected to obtain explicit consent from clients to the best execution policy. Rather, this policy should be provided to clients at the onboarding stage and added to the firm’s website (MiFID disclosures). A client’s continued relationship with the firm confirms that they have no issues with the policy. If a client does not consent to the policy, then they will simply cease to undertake business/enter into transactions with the firm. 

Firms must review their order execution policies whenever a material event occurs, but at least annually, and must notify clients of any material changes to their order execution arrangements or execution policy. However, the FCA does not define the term ‘material change’, rather it is for firms to determine whether any changes to their policy requires them to advise clients. 

## 14.12.3 Specific Client Instructions 

When a firm receives a specific instruction from a client, it must execute the order as instructed. It will be deemed to have satisfied its obligation to obtain the best possible result if it follows such specific instructions (even if an alternative means of executing the order would have given a better result). 

Firms should not induce clients to instruct an order in a particular way, by expressly indicating or implicitly suggesting the content of the instruction to the client, if they know that any instruction to the client will have the likely effect of preventing it from obtaining the best possible result for the client. 

247 

## 14.12.4 Compliance with Policies, and the Obligations on Portfolio Managers and Firms Receiving/Transmitting Orders 

Firms must monitor the effectiveness of their execution arrangements and policies to identify and (if need be) correct any deficiencies. 

In addition, they must be able to demonstrate to their clients, on request, that they have executed their orders in accordance with their execution policy. 

Portfolio managers must act in their clients’ best interests when placing orders for them, on the basis of the firm’s investment decisions. 

Firms receiving and transmitting orders for clients must also act in their client’s best interests when transmitting those orders to other parties (eg, brokers) to execute. This means taking account of the execution factors listed above (unless the client has given specific instructions, in which case these must be followed). Portfolio managers, and receivers or transmitters of orders, must also maintain order execution policies, but need not get client consent to them. 

The policy must identify, in respect of each class of instrument, the entities with which the orders are placed or to which they transmit orders for execution. The entities must have execution arrangements that enable the firm to comply with its obligations under the best execution requirements when it places an order with, or transmits an order to, that entity for execution. 

## 14.12.5 Client Order Handling 

Firms must apply procedures and arrangements which provide for the prompt, fair and expeditious execution of client orders, relative to the other orders or trading interests of the firm and have in place an appropriate policy. This rule is also consistent with the need for firms to avoid conflicts of interest, if possible. 

In particular, these should allow comparable client orders to be executed in the order in which they are received. 

Therefore, firms should ensure that: 

- executed client orders are promptly and accurately recorded and allocated 

- comparable orders are executed sequentially and promptly, unless this is impracticable, or client interests require otherwise 

- retail clients are informed of any material difficulty in the prompt execution of their order, promptly on the firm becoming aware of this, and 

- if the firm is responsible for overseeing or arranging settlement, that the assets or money are delivered promptly and correctly. 

Firms must not misuse information relating to client orders and must also take steps to prevent its abuse (for example, in order to profit by dealing for its own account). 

248 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

Firms’ Order Handling Policy will deal with orders left by a client which state ‘good till cancelled’ or ‘good to the close’. In these circumstances, firms should discuss and agree with the client what their understanding of such a restriction placed on an order means – as firms, markets, or jurisdictions may have different interpretations of the terms. 

## 14.12.6 Aggregation and Allocation 

Firms may aggregate their own-account deals with those of a client, or aggregate two or more clients’ deals, only where: 

- it is unlikely to disadvantage any of the aggregated clients 

- the fact that **aggregation** may work to their disadvantage is disclosed to the clients, or 

- an order allocation policy has been established which provides (in sufficiently precise terms) for the fair allocation of transactions; this must cover how volume and price of orders will affect allocation, and it must also cover how partial allocations will be dealt with. 

When an aggregated order is only partly executed, the firm must then allocate the various trades in order with this allocation policy. 

When firms have own-account deals in an aggregated order along with those of clients, they must not allocate them in a way which is detrimental to the clients. 

In particular, a firm must allocate the client orders in priority over its own, unless it can show that without the inclusion of its own order, less favourable terms would have been obtained. In these circumstances, it may allocate the deals proportionately. 

The firm’s order allocation policy must incorporate procedures preventing the reallocation of ownaccount orders aggregated with client orders in a way detrimental to a client. 

## 14.12.7 Client Limit Orders 

Unless the client instructs otherwise, a firm which receives a client limit order for shares listed on a Regulated Market, and which cannot immediately execute it under the prevailing market conditions, must make the limit order public (in a manner easily accessible to other market participants) immediately so that it can be executed as soon as possible. It need not do so, however, for orders over normal market size. 

It may do this by: 

- transmitting the order to a Regulated Market or MTF operating an order book trading system, or 

- ensuring the order is made public and can be easily executed as soon as market conditions allow. 

249 

## 14.12.8 The Personal Account Dealing Rules – Application and Purpose 

The personal account dealing rules apply to firms that conduct designated investment business. These rules require firms to establish, implement and maintain adequate arrangements aimed at preventing certain activities when: 

- these activities may give rise to conflicts of interest 

- the individual involved in these activities has access to inside information, as defined in the UK MAR, and 

- the individual involved in these activities has access to other confidential information relating to clients or transactions with or for clients. 

The arrangements should aim to prevent the following activities: 

## 1. Entering into a personal transaction that is: 

   - contrary to UK MAR 

   - involves misuse or improper disclosure of confidential information, or 

   - conflicts with the firm’s duties to a customer. 

2. Improperly advising or procuring so that anyone else enters into a transaction that (if it had been done by the employee himself) would have fallen foul of point 1 above or a relevant provision. 

3. Improperly disclosing information or opinion, if he knows, or should know, that the person to whom he has disclosed it is likely to enter into a transaction that (if it had been done by the employee himself) would have fallen foul of point 1 above or of a relevant provision, or encouraging someone else to do so. 

The relevant provisions are the rules on: 

- Personal account transactions undertaken by financial analysts contained elsewhere in COBS 

- The misuse of information relating to pending client orders (section 14.12.5 of this chapter). 

Firms must keep records of all personal transactions notified to them, and of any authorisation or prohibition made in connection with them. 

## 14.12.9 Compliance with, and Exceptions to, the Personal Account Dealing Rules 

The arrangements must ensure that the affected employees are aware of the restrictions on personal transactions, and of the firm’s procedures in this regard. They must be such that the firm is informed promptly of any such personal transaction, either by notification of it, or some other procedure enabling the firm to identify it. 

When outsourcing takes place, the arrangements must be such that the outsource must maintain a record of personal transactions undertaken by any relevant person, and provide this record to the firm promptly on request. 

250 

PRA/FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

The rules on personal account dealing are disapplied for: 

- deals under a discretionary management service, if there is no prior communication between the portfolio manager and the relevant person (or any other person for whose account the transaction is being executed) about the deal, and 

- deals in units or shares in certain classes of fund, if the relevant person (and any other person for whom the deals are effected) is not involved in the management of the fund. 

## 14.12.10 Churning and Switching 

**Churning** is the activity of dealing excessively frequently for a client, in order to generate additional fees and/or commissions for the firm. It is relevant when, for example, a firm manages a client’s portfolio on a discretionary basis. Switching is the activity of selling one investment and replacing it with another. 

The FCA’s guidance on churning and switching is contained in the COBS rules on suitability. It states that, in the context of assessing suitability, a series of transactions that look suitable in isolation may not be suitable if the recommendations or trading decisions to make them are so frequent as to be detrimental to the client. 

It also states that firms should bear the client’s investment strategy in mind when determining how frequently to deal for him. 

## 14.13 Underwriting and Placing (COBS 11A) 

Firms who provide the investment service of ‘underwriting’ and ‘placing’ (ie, bringing new issues to the market), must provide clients (issuers) with certain information before accepting a mandate to manage a new offering. 

Investment firms that provide advice on corporate finance strategy, and provide the service of underwriting or placing of financial instruments, shall, before accepting a mandate to manage the offering, have arrangements in place to inform the issuer client of the following: 

- a. the various financing alternatives available with the firm, and an indication of the amount of transaction fees associated with each alternative 

- b. the timing and the process with regard to the corporate finance advice on pricing of the offering 

- c. the timing and the process with regard to the corporate finance advice on placing of the offering 

- d. details of the targeted investors, to whom the firm intends to offer the financial instruments 

- e. the job titles and departments of the relevant individuals involved in the provision of corporate finance advice on the price and allotment of financial instruments, and 

- f. the firm’s arrangements to prevent or manage conflicts of interest that may arise where the firm places the relevant financial instruments with its investment clients or with its own proprietary book. 

Firms are required to be able to identify all underwriting and placing operations and ensure that adequate controls are in place to manage any potential conflicts of interest. They must have in place a centralised process to identify all underwriting and placing operations and record such information, including the date on which the firm was informed of potential underwriting and placing operations. 

251 

Firms must be able to identify all potential conflicts of interest arising from other activities of the investment firm, or group, and implement appropriate management procedures. In cases where an investment firm cannot prevent or manage a conflict of interest by way of implementing appropriate procedures, the investment firm shall not engage in the operation. 

Firms must have in place systems, controls and procedures to identify and prevent or manage conflicts of interest that arise in relation to possible under-pricing or over-pricing of an issue or involvement of relevant parties in the process. 

In particular, firms must as a minimum requirement establish, implement and maintain internal arrangements to ensure both of the following: 

- a. that the pricing of the offering does not promote the interests of other clients or the firm’s own interests, in a way that may conflict with the issuer client’s interests, and 

- b. the prevention or management of a situation where persons responsible for providing services to the firm’s investment clients are directly involved in decisions about corporate finance advice on pricing to the issuer client. 

Firms must provide clients with information about how the recommendation as to the price of the offering and the timings involved is determined. 

## 14.14 Investment Research (COBS 12) 

## 14.14.1 Conflicts of Interest in Relation to Investment Research 

Conflicts management rules on the production and dissemination of investment research apply to all firms, especially where they fall under UK MAR. 

Refer to chapter 3, section 3, for further details. 

## 14.15 Reporting Information to Clients (COBS 16 and 16A) 

## 14.15.1 Confirmation of Transactions and Periodic Statements – Reporting to Clients 

Firms are required to ensure that clients receive adequate reports on the services they provide to them. These must also include any associated costs. 

If a firm (other than one managing investments) carries out an order for a client, it must: 

- provide the essential information on it, promptly, and in a durable medium (if the transaction relates to derivatives and options) 

- provide the client with information about the status of their orders on request, and 

- send a ‘notice’ confirming the deal details as soon as possible (but no later than on the next business day); when the confirmation is received from a third party, the firm must pass the details on no later than the business day following receipt. 

252 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

The ‘notice’ to be provided to the client should include the following information: 

- identification of the firm 

- name and/or designation of the client 

- trading day and time 

- type of order (eg, limit order/market order/client-specific instructions) 

- venue where the transaction was executed 

- financial instrument 

- buy/sell indicator 

- nature of the order if other than buy/sell (ie, exercise of an option) 

- unit price 

- total consideration 

- total of the commissions, expenses and any mark-up/-down applied to the transaction 

- rate of exchange where the transaction involves the conversion of funds from one currency to another in order to settle the transaction in the client’s base currency, and 

- client’s responsibility in respect of settlement of the transaction. 

Firms do not need to send the required information where the client is receiving the same required information from another person. 

For non-MiFID business only, there are some exceptions to the above rules and requirements, namely if the client has confirmed that confirmations need not be sent either in general or in specified circumstances. 

Firms must keep copies of all confirmations sent to clients: 

- for MiFID business, for at least five years from the date of dispatch, and 

- for other business, for at least three years from the date of dispatch. 

For the purpose of calculating the unit price in the trade confirmation information, if the order is executed in tranches, firms may supply clients with the information about the price of each tranche or the average price. 

## 14.15.2 Confirmation of Transactions and Periodic Statements – Periodic Reporting 

Firms managing investments on behalf of clients must provide them with a periodic statement in a durable medium, unless these are provided by another party. For retail clients, this must be at least quarterly, with the following exceptions: 

- if the client receives deal-by-deal confirmations, and certain higher-risk investments are excluded, the statement may be sent every 12 months, and 

- if the client has authorised that his portfolio be leveraged, the statement must be provided monthly. 

A firm must make and retain a copy of any periodic statement: 

- for MiFID business, for a period of at least five years, or 

- for other business, for a period of at least three years. 

253 

If firms manage investments for clients or operate certain types of account for them which include uncovered open positions in a contingent liability transaction, they must report any losses over a preagreed limit to the client. They must do so by the end of the business day on which the limit is breached. If this happens on a non-business day, they must do so by the end of the next business day. 

A contingent liability transaction is one that involves any actual or potential liability for the client that exceeds the cost of acquiring the instrument. For the purpose of calculating the unit price in the trade confirmation information or periodic information, if the order is executed in tranches, firms may supply clients with the information about the price of each tranche or the average price. 

## 14.16 Specialist Regimes (COBS 18) 

This chapter of COBS seeks to identify the relevant parts of COBS that do not apply and are most likely not to apply to firms undertaking the following activities: 

- Trustee firms. 

- Energy market activity and oil market activity. 

- Corporate finance business. 

- Stock lending activity. 

- Residual CIS operators and small authorised UK AIFMs. 

- UCITS management companies. 

- Full scope UK AIFMs and incoming EEA AIFM branches. 

- Lloyd’s. 

- Insurance special purpose vehicles (ISPVs). 

- Depositaries. 

- ICVCs (investment companies with variable capital). 

- UCITS qualifiers, AIFM qualifiers and service companies. 

- Authorised professional firms. 

- Operating an electronic system in relation to lending. 

Prior to the implementation of UK MiFID, the UK applied fewer prescriptive provisions in terms of the application of conduct of business requirements to firms undertaking this type of business activity. 

For each section of COBS 18 the Sourcebook seeks to highlight the applicable parts of COBS that do not apply – based on the activity in question, as well as highlighting certain rules that are unlikely to apply. 

## 15. Client Assets Sourcebook (CASS) 

The rules relating to the custody and safeguarding of client money and assets are contained in the FCA’s ‘CASS’ Sourcebook. The CASS rules are designed to ensure that firms take adequate steps to protect those client assets for which they are responsible. 

Within CASS, the requirement to segregate client money from a firm’s own money is aimed at ensuring that, if the firm fails, client money will not be used to repay its creditors. Usually this is done by ensuring that it is placed promptly in a designated client money account with a bank and ensuring that the bank treats client money as separate from the firm’s own money. 

254 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

CASS, in general, applies to every firm, with some specific exemptions. It applies directly in respect of activities conducted with, or for, all categories of client, ie, retail clients, professional clients and eligible counterparties. 

**CASS 6** contains the custody rules that apply when a firm holds financial instruments for a client in the course of MiFID business, and when it is safeguarding and administering investments in the course of non-MiFID business. 

**CASS 7** contains the client money rules, applying to a UK MiFID investment firm either when: 

- it holds client money in the course of its UK MiFID business 

- in the course of investment business that is non-UK MiFID business in respect of any investment agreement entered into, or to be entered into with or for a client, or 

- it holds money in respect of which **CASS 5** – Client money: insurance mediation activity applies, and the firm elects to apply the provisions of CASS 7. 

Firms must, when holding safe custody assets belonging to clients, make adequate arrangements so as to safeguard clients’ ownership rights, especially in the event of the firm’s insolvency and to prevent the use of safe custody assets belonging to a client on the firm’s own account, except with the client’s express consent. Firms must also introduce adequate organisational arrangements to minimise the risk of loss or diminution of client’s safe custody assets. When a firm accepts client money, it must take the necessary steps to ensure that the client money is deposited in accordance with the requirement of CASS 7.13.3 (depositing client money) in that it is held in an account or accounts identified separately from any accounts used to hold money belonging to the firm. 

CASS 7.13.3 stipulates that a firm, on receiving any client money, must promptly place this money into one or more accounts opened with any of the following: 

- a central bank 

- a Banking Consolidation Directive (BCD) credit institution 

- a bank authorised in a third country, or 

- a qualifying money market fund. 

There are a number of circumstances when the client money rules will not apply, for example, when money is held in connection with a delivery versus payment (DVP) transaction (unless the DVP does not occur by the close of business on the third business day following the date of payment of a delivery obligation), or when it becomes due and payable to the firm. Banks holding monies as deposits with themselves are also exempted. 

Following the global financial crisis of 2007–08 a number of insolvency appointments resulted in changes to the CASS requirements. Although the UK client asset regime has performed relatively well in facilitating the early return of client assets and money (compared with some overseas jurisdictions), the failure of Lehman Brothers and the financial crisis in general highlighted a number of issues relating to existing market practices. 

255 

Changes made to the CASS rules included the following issues: 

- **Increased reporting to clients** – daily reporting on client money and assets holdings to all prime brokerage clients. This means that clients know exactly what is happening to their assets, what transactions have been completed and, if relevant, which and how many of their assets have been re-hypothecated. 

- **Holding client money with group banks** – restricting the placement of client money deposits held in client bank accounts within a group to 20%. This limits the amount by which a client is exposed to group credit risk. The rules apply at an entity level if it is a BCD credit institution, a bank authorised in a third-country, a qualified money market fund or the entity operating or managing a qualifying money market fund and a member of the same group as the firm. 

- **Prohibiting the use of general liens in custodial agreements** . 

- **Creating a new controlled function with specific responsibility for client money and assets** – a senior individual within the firm should be responsible for oversight and protection of client assets and money. Proportionate to the size of the firm, this should be one named individual who will be subject to the SM&CR Certification Regime (they would not normally be a Senior Manager under the SM&CR Regime). 

- **Introducing a Client Money and Assets Return (CMAR)** – a monthly return for large firms and medium sized firms, providing the FCA with an overview of firm-specific CASS holdings – enabling the FCA to make regulated interventions on a firm-specific or thematic basis. There is no requirement for small sized firms to submit a CMAR return. 

## 15.1 Due Diligence and the Establishment of Client Bank Accounts 

Firms that do not deposit client money with a central bank must exercise all due skill, care and diligence in the selection, appointment and periodic review of the credit institution, bank or qualifying money market fund where the money is deposited, and in the arrangements for the holding of this money. 

Firms must keep a record of the grounds upon which they satisfy themselves as to the appropriateness of their selection of a credit institution, a bank or a qualifying money market fund. The firm must make the record on the date it makes the selection and must keep it from the date of such selection until five years after the firm ceases to use the third party to hold client money. 

## 15.2 Reconciliation of Client Assets 

A firm must keep such records and accounts as necessary to enable it, at any time and without delay, to distinguish safe custody assets held for one client from safe custody assets held for any other client, and from the firm’s own assets. 

CASS 6.6 sets out the obligations on firms to perform internal and external reconciliations. Broadly, reconciliations should be undertaken, as often as necessary, to ensure the accuracy of a firm’s records and accounts, between its internal accounts and those records of third parties who hold the assets in safe custody. If possible, they should be done by someone who has not been involved in the production or maintenance of the records that are being reconciled. 

256 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

If the reconciliation shows a discrepancy, the firm must make good (or provide the equivalent of) any shortfall for which it is responsible. If another person is responsible, the firm should take reasonable steps to resolve the position with that person. 

Firms are required to inform the FCA without delay of any failure to comply with the reconciliation requirements, including reconciliation discrepancies and making good any such differences. 

## 15.3 Reconciliation of Client Money 

The main purpose for firms to carry out reconciliations is to ensure that their internal records of client money held matches the actual amount of client money held. 

CASS 7.15 sets out the obligations of firms to perform internal and external reconciliations. 

## 15.3.1 Internal Client Money Reconciliations 

As explained in CASS 7.12.1R, in order to comply with its obligations under CASS 7.15.5R (Records, accounts and reconciliations), and when relevant SYSC 4.1.1R (General requirements) and SYSC 6.1.1R (Compliance), firms must carry out internal reconciliations of records and accounts of client money the firm holds in client bank accounts and client transaction accounts. 

The following methods of reconciliation are deemed to be appropriate for these purposes by the FCA (the standard method of internal client money reconciliation). 

- Each business day, a firm that adopts the normal approach should check whether its client money resource, being the aggregate balance on the firm’s client bank accounts, as at the close of business on the previous business day, was at least equal to the client money requirement as at the close of business on that day. 

- Each business day, a firm that adopts the alternative approach should ensure that its client money resource, being the aggregate balance on the firm’s client bank accounts, as at the close of business on that business day is at least equal to the client money requirement as at the close of business on the previous business day. No excess or shortfall should arise when adopting the alternative approach. 

For the purposes of performing its reconciliations of records and accounts, a firm should use the values contained in its accounting records, for example its cash book, rather than values contained in statements received. 

If a reconciliation shows a discrepancy, the firm must investigate to identify the reason for the discrepancy and ensure that either any shortfall is paid into the client bank account, or any excess is withdrawn from the client bank account by close of business on the day the reconciliation is performed. 

## 15.3.2 External Client Money Reconciliations 

This means cross-checking the internal client money accounts against the records of third parties (eg, banks) with whom client money is held. Firms must perform external reconciliations as often as is necessary, and as soon as reasonably practicable after the date to which the reconciliation relates. 

257 

If there is a discrepancy, the firm must investigate and correct it as soon as possible. If it cannot do so, and the firm should be holding a greater amount of client money, it must pay its own money into the client bank account pending resolution of the discrepancy, which it must correct as soon as possible. 

If a firm has not complied with these requirements or is for some reason unable to comply in a material aspect with a particular requirement, it must inform the FCA in writing. 

The FCA believes that an adequate method of reconciling client money balances with external records is as follows: 

- a reconciliation of a client bank account as recorded by the firm with the statement issued by the bank (or other form of confirmation issued by the bank), and 

- a reconciliation of the balance on each client transaction account as recorded by the firm, with the balance of that account as set out in the statement (or other form of confirmation) issued by the person with whom the account is held. 

## 15.3.3 Exemption from CASS 

CASS does not apply to, inter alia: 

- ICVCs 

- UCITS qualifying schemes 

- a credit institution under the BCD, in relation to deposits held with itself (held as ‘banker’ not as ‘trustee’) 

- coins held for the value of their metal 

- money transferred under title transfer collateral arrangements 

- money held in connection with a DVP transaction (unless payment does not occur after three business days) 

- money due and payable to the firm 

- if a firm carries on business in its name but on behalf of the client when that is required by the nature of the transaction and the client is in agreement, or 

- the custody rules (CASS 6) do not apply if a client transfers full ownership of a safe custody asset to a firm for the purpose of securing or otherwise covering present or future, actual contingent or prospective obligations. 

Specific rules within CASS may be disapplied depending on the nature of a firm’s activities. The details are set out within the individual rules. 

There are specific requirements that firms must comply with in order to cease to treat any unclaimed balances as client money or assets which is allocated to an individual client. Firms are able to pay away such balances to a registered charity, providing that the firm can demonstrate that it has taken reasonable steps to trace the clients concerned and return the balance, and has held the balance concerned for at least six years in respect of client money and 12 years for clients’ assets. 

If a firm wishes to make use of the new requirements, then the governing body must approve of this and unconditionally undertake to pay to the clients concerned a sum equal to the balance paid away to charity in the event of the client seeking to claim the balance in the future. 

258 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

Reasonable steps that a firm must undertake in the course of being able to demonstrate it has taken ‘reasonable steps’ in respect of client money and client assets include: 

- determining the correct contact details for the client, and 

- writing to the client at the last known address either by post or by electronic mail (e-mail). 

Firms may also use any available means to determine the correct contact details for the client, including telephoning the client, searching internal records, media advertising, searching public records, mortality screening, using credit reference agencies or tracing agencies. 

## 15.3.4 CASS Audit 

If a firm holds client money and assets, then it must have an annual CASS assurance audit performed. The audit assurance report is submitted to the FCA. The Supervision Manual (SUP) determines the type of report that must be completed. 

Where a firm claims that it does not hold client assets, the external auditor will be required to complete a limited assurance client assets report. The auditor must state whether anything has come to its attention that causes it to believe that the firm held client money or custody assets during the period covered by the report. 

The Financial Reporting Council (FRC) is the body responsible for setting the standard for CASS audit reviews. The FRC has developed a ‘Client Asset Assurance Standard’ (Standard) which establishes requirements and provides guidance for CASS auditors reporting to the FCA in accordance with its SUP rules in respect of engagements that involve evaluating and reporting on a regulated firm’s (firm) compliance with the FCA’s CASS rules and other rules relevant to the holding of client assets. 

The Standard establishes requirements with respect to: 

- a. the process for forming, and the expression of, reasonable assurance opinions 

- b. the process for forming, and the expression of, limited assurance opinions 

- c. the provision of reasonable assurance to the FCA with respect to a firm’s proposed adoption of: 

   1. the alternative approach to client money segregation 

   2. a non-standard method of client money reconciliation, and 

- d. CASS auditor confirmations in respect of non-statutory client money trusts. 

The key objectives of the CASS Assurance Standard are to: 

- a. improve the quality of CASS audits and other CASS assurance engagements 

- b. adequately support and challenge CASS auditors when undertaking CASS assurance engagements and, in particular, to define the nature and extent of the work effort required for both reasonable assurance and limited assurance CASS assurance engagements without undermining the importance of the CASS auditor’s judgement 

- c. support the objectives of the FCA’s client assets regime regarding the effective safekeeping of client assets and client monies and in particular to guard against systemic failure of the CASS regime 

259 

- d. manage the expectations of: 

   1. the management of firms that hold client assets, and 

   2. third-party administrators (when a CASS auditor is engaged to provide assurance to the FCA on client assets that it handles or accounts for) 

- e. support the effective training of CASS auditors by both the accounting bodies and other training organisations 

- f. help to establish realistic expectations regarding the integrity of the UK client assets regime with the beneficial owners of client assets, and 

- g. underpin the effectiveness of the FRC’s enforcement and disciplinary activities with respect to CASS assurance engagements. 

Prior to starting an audit, the CASS auditor would need to obtain an understanding of the firm’s business model and permissions it has received from the FCA. 

The CASS auditor must gain an understanding of the major activities that the firm uses to monitor internal controls relevant to its compliance with the CASS rules and how the firm initiates remedial actions in response to deficiencies in its controls. Where the firm has an internal audit or compliance function, which covers those major activities intended to ensure compliance with the CASS rules, the CASS auditor shall perform or obtain, as appropriate, and document: 

- an evaluation of whether the function’s organisational status and relevant policies and procedures adequately support the objectivity of the internal auditors, or compliance function 

- an understanding of the nature of the internal audit or compliance function’s responsibilities with respect to client assets 

- an assessment of whether the function applies a systematic and disciplined approach, including quality control, and 

- the activities the function has performed or intends to perform with respect to client assets. 

The CASS auditor shall consider how the design of control activities: 

- enables the firm to identify where client assets may arise in the business 

- seeks to ensure that client assets are segregated and safeguarded effectively 

- addresses the performance of internal and external reconciliations as required by the CASS rules, and 

- addresses the establishment and acknowledgement of trust status over client assets. 

The CASS auditor shall further evaluate whether there is adequate segregation of duties and if the design of the system incorporates sufficiently robust controls over system changes. The auditor must give an opinion on the following, as well as stating/identifying where the applicable requirements have or have not been met: 

- The firm has maintained systems adequate to enable it to comply with the custody rules, the collateral rules, the client money rules and the mandate rules throughout the period. 

- The firm was in compliance with the custody rules, the collateral rules, the client money rules and the mandate rules at the date at which the report was made. 

- In the case of an investment management firm, personal investment firm, a UCITS firm, securities and futures firm, a firm acting as trustee or depositary of an AIF, a firm acting as trustee or depositary of a UCITS or IFPRU (Prudential Sourcebook for Investment Firms) investment firm, or MIFIDPRU 

260 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

(Prudential Sourcebook for MiFID investment firms) firm, when a subsidiary of the firm is, during the period, a nominee company in whose name custody assets of the firm are registered, that nominee company has maintained throughout the period systems for the custody, identification and control of custody assets which adequately included reconciliations at appropriate intervals between the records maintained (whether by the firm or the nominee company) and statements or confirmations from custodians or from the person who maintained the record of legal entitlement. 

- If there has been a secondary pooling event during the period, the firm has complied with the relevant client money distribution rules in relation to that pooling event. 

The CASS auditor will provide a ‘nil’ return where there have been no breaches identified. 

The client assets report is made up of two parts: 

- Part 1 is the auditor’s opinion, and 

- Part 2 is a breaches schedule. 

The FCA expects that the list of breaches will include every breach of a rule in CASS insofar as that rule is within the scope of the client assets report and is identified in the course of the auditor’s review of the period covered by the report, whether identified by the auditor or disclosed to it by the firm, or by any third-party. 

For the purpose of determining whether to qualify its opinion or express an adverse opinion, the FCA would expect an auditor to exercise its professional judgment as to the significance of a rule breach, as well as to its context, duration and incidence of repetition. The FCA would expect an auditor to consider the aggregate effect of any breaches when judging whether a firm has failed to comply with the requirements described above. 

Under FSMA (Communications by Auditors) Regulations 2001, CASS auditors have duties in certain circumstances to make reports to the FCA, including reporting breaches of the FCA’s rules that it reasonably believes may be of material significance to the FCA. Therefore, where a CASS auditor becomes aware of such breaches, it does not wait to report them to the FCA by means of the breaches schedule that it appends to its routine assurance reports to the FCA. 

The consequence of regulatory failure is the potential to be censored by the FCA, and depending upon the nature and seriousness of the regulatory failure, the penalty could lead to a fine. 

## Review of Auditors’ Client Assets Reports 

Firms are required to review the ‘draft’ client assets report and where necessary provide an explanation of the circumstances that gave rise to each of the breaches identified in the draft report, and any remedial actions that they have undertaken or plan to undertake to correct those breaches. 

The FCA expects firms to use the client assets report as a tool to evaluate the effectiveness of the systems that they have in place for the purpose of complying with the CASS requirements. 

The auditor must deliver to the firm the final report within four months of the end of the period covered. 

261 

## 15.3.5 Rights of Use 

The ‘rights of use’ is an arrangement under which a client transfers full ownership of assets to a collateraltaker (ie, an investment firm) for the purpose of securing or otherwise covering the performance associated with relevant financial obligations. 

As there is a transfer of full ownership of assets to a firm, they cease to be client assets and so are not subject to CASS requirements. The exception to this is if the money and assets belong to a retail client and the purpose of title transfer is to secure or cover that client’s present or future, actual, contingent or prospective obligations under a **contract for difference (CFD)** or a contract that is a future and that is entered into with a firm acting as market maker. 

Title transfer collateral arrangements (TTCAs) are normally used by firms that undertake derivatives business, where the firms enter into margined transactions with clients. These arrangements allow those firms to treat margin (the amount paid by the client as collateral to open the position or bet) as their own working capital, rather than client money (which must be segregated). Often, those firms subsequently use that money to fund their hedging arrangements. 

## 15.3.6 Rehypothecation 

Rehypothecation is an alternative name for repledging – in other words, a party that receives a pledge of collateral and then pledges the same collateral to a third party. In practice this is operated by brokers/ prime brokers for hedge fund clients, where typically the hedge funds will post collateral against loans that they have taken out and the collateral is used by the broker/prime broker for its own purposes. The benefit to the hedge fund is lower costs of financing. 

In the repo and derivatives market, rehypothecation is often referred to as ‘reuse’. 

The collapse of both Lehman Brothers and MF Global highlights the pitfalls of clients allowing the firm to reuse their assets as its own. In both cases internal records and accounts were incomplete and it was difficult to identify which assets had been pledged to the firm by which clients and who was owed what. Given the issues that the collapse of Lehman Brothers raised, where firms’ collateral was frozen on a global basis while the authorities tried to unwind/unpick who was owed what, it is surprising that in 2011 the same thing happened again to clients of MF Global – who had sold/repledged over £10 billion of customer collateral. 

In both cases, clients had to fight to regain assets pledged, because they were treated as unsecured creditors. This also highlighted inadequate record keeping, systems and controls at the firms. 

## 15.3.7 Bare Security Interests 

If a client has lodged collateral with a firm and it is provided on the provision that the collateral is just pledged or a charge is placed on the assets, the firm is not permitted to reuse the assets. 

The client remains the legal owner of those assets until the bare security interest is exercised or crystallises on the client’s default. 

262 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

## 15.3.8 CASS Resolution Pack 

A CASS Resolution Pack will enable firms that hold client money and client assets to be able to maintain and retrieve certain documents and information relating to the assets and cash that they held on behalf of clients in the event of a crisis, such as insolvency. Firms must maintain their CASS Resolution Pack at all times. 

Firms must be able to retrieve documents required under the CASS resolution pack as soon as practicable, no later than within 48 hours. 

The purpose of the CASS resolution pack is to assist an insolvency practitioner in returning client money and custody assets to clients in a timely manner should the firms cease to trade. The insolvency of Lehman Brothers International (Europe) and MF Global UK highlighted difficulties in returning client assets in a timely manner and determining who had control over the clients’ assets. 

Firms subject to CASS 6 (Custody rules) and CASS 7 (Client money rules) will be required to produce and maintain a CASS resolution pack. Firms are not required to complete a pack if they only hold client money for insurance mediation business (under CASS 5) and if they only arrange safeguarding and administration of assets. 

Firms will need adequate internal procedures and processes for monitoring the content of their CASS resolution pack. If there are changes to processes and procedures, these will need to be updated in the pack within five days of the change occurring. 

The individual performing the CASS operational oversight function must report annually to the firm’s board in respect of compliance with the CASS resolution pack requirements. If a firm is unable to comply with the CASS resolution pack requirements then it must notify the FCA immediately. 

## Contents of a CASS Resolution Pack 

The following documents and information must be immediately retrievable: 

- **Institutions** – identification of the institution appointed to hold client money, to hold or control client money in a client transaction account or for the deposit of safe custody assets. 

- **Individuals** – identifies each senior manager and director and any other individual and the nature of their responsibility within the firm who are critical/important to the performance of operational functions related to any regulatory obligations, and the individual to whom the firm has appointed the carrying out/performance of the CASS operational oversight function. 

- **Agreement with institutions** – a copy of the executed agreement, including any side letters or other agreements used to clarify/modify the terms of the executed agreement, between the firm and institutions, that relate to the holding of client money or safe custody assets – including written notification or trust acknowledgement letters sent/received. 

- **Custody assets** – the most recent internal and external reconciliation relating to safe custody assets. 

- • **Client money** – the most recent internal and external reconciliation relating to client money. The following documents and information must be retrievable as soon as possible, but within no later than 48 hours. 

- **Master documents** – containing information sufficient to retrieve each document in the firm’s CASS resolution pack. 

263 

- **Agents** – documents identifying each appointed representative, tied agent, field representative or other agent of the firm which received client money or safe custody assets in its capacity as the firm’s agent. 

- **Group structure** – documents identifying and providing certain other information with respect to each group member and/or third party (a ‘third-party outsourcer’) involved in the performance of operational aspects of the firm’s obligations under the client money/client assets rules. 

- **Service providers** – copies of each executed agreement, side letter and/or amendment agreement with any third-party outsourcer. 

- **Access to service providers** – documents which describe how to: 

   - a. gain access to relevant information held by, and 

- b. effect a transfer of client money or safe custody assets held by, each third-party outsourcer. 

- • **Procedures** – copy of the firm manual in which procedures for the management, recording and transfer of client money and safe custody assets that the firm holds are found. 

- **Due diligence** – records of the grounds upon which the firm satisfied itself of the appropriateness of its selection of each third party with which it deposits safe custody assets and client money. 

- **Client agreements** – if safe custody assets have been used for securities financing transactions, a copy of the consent from clients to use the assets and the assets used (number/type). 

- **Records and accounts** – documents which enable the firm to distinguish client money and safe custody assets of one client from that of another client or the firm’s own money. 

- **Reconciliation methods** – explanation of the methods of internal reconciliation of client money balances. 

- **Client categorisation and agreements** – copies of all agreements with retail and professional clients, records of client categorisation and supporting documentation and relevant documentation relating to recategorising of a client. 

## 15.3.9 Mandate 

A mandate is required from the client when the client is giving the firm control over their assets and/ or liabilities. A mandate is required if a firm is undertaking designated investment business (including MiFID business), insurance mediation activity (except if it relates to a reinsurance contract) and debt management activity. In most instances, the mandate will be provided to the firm through the firm’s terms of business. 

Where a client has a mandate with a firm over the control of their assets held at a third party, the client money (CASS 7) and custody rules (CASS 6) will not apply. A mandate is any means that gives the firm the ability to control a client’s assets or liabilities, which meet the conditions in (1) to (6) below: 

1. They are held at a third party. 

2. They are obtained by the firm from the client, and with the client’s consent. 

3. If those means are obtained in the course of, or in connection with, the firm’s insurance mediation activity, they are in written form at the time they are obtained from the client. 

4. They are retained by the firm. 

5. They put the firm in a position where it is able to give any or all of the types of instructions described in (a) to (d): 

   - a. instructions to another person in relation to the client’s money that is credited to an account maintained by that other person for the client 

264 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

   - b. instructions to another person in relation to any money to which the client has an entitlement, if that other person is responsible to the client for that entitlement (including if that other person is holding client money for the client) 

   - c. instructions to another person in relation to an asset of the client, if that other person is responsible to the client for holding that asset (including if that other person is safeguarding and administering investments, acting as trustee or depositary of an AIF or acting as trustee or depositary of a UCITS), and 

   - d. instructions to another person such that the client incurs a debt or other liability to that other person or any other person (other than the firm). 

6. Their circumstances are such that the client’s further involvement would not be necessary for the firm’s instructions described in 5(a) to 4(d) to be given effect. 

The FCA has defined a mandate as ‘any means that give a firm the ability to control a client’s assets or liabilities’. 

The mandate rules require firms to establish and maintain records and internal controls to prevent any misuse of the mandate authority granted by the client. 

A mandate can take any form and need not state that it is a mandate. It can be a stand-alone document containing certain information conferring authority to control a client’s assets or liabilities on the firm, a specific provision within a document or agreement that also relates to other matters, or can be provided by a client verbally or be in paperless form. 

A firm must establish and maintain adequate records and internal controls in respect of its use of the mandates. This would include: 

- an up-to-date list of each mandate that the firm has obtained, including a record of any conditions placed by the client or the firm’s management on the use of the mandate and, where a mandate was received in non-written form in the course of, or in connection with, its designated investment business, the details required under CASS 8.3.2C R 

- a record of each transaction entered into by the firm for each mandate 

- internal controls to ensure that each transaction entered into by the firm is carried out in accordance with any conditions established by the client or the firm 

- details of the procedures and internal controls around the giving of instructions under the mandates that the firm has, and 

- where the firm holds a passbook or similar document belonging to the client, internal controls for the safeguarding (including against loss, unauthorised destruction, theft, fraud or misuse) of any passbook or similar document belonging to the client held by the firm. 

The required list of mandates that a firm must maintain should be in a durable medium that allows storage of information in a way accessible for future reference by the regulator or by an auditor preparing a client money report. The record must also be able to be updated and amended where necessary, with the ability to ascertain the details of the mandate list prior to changes/updates being made. 

265 

If the instruction/authority for the mandate was provided verbally by the client, then the entry on the mandate list must contain the following information: 

- the nature of the mandate (eg, debit card details, direct debits) 

- the purpose of the mandate (eg, collecting insurance premiums) 

- how the mandate was obtained (eg, by telephone) 

- the name of the relevant client, and 

- the date on which the mandate was obtained. 

Firms must retain records for each mandate for the following periods after they cease to have the mandate: 

- for a minimum of five years if the relevant mandate was held by the firm in the course of, or in connection with, its UK MiFID business, and 

- a minimum of one year for all other instances. 

## 16. Supervision (SUP) 

## 16.1 Applications to Vary and Cancel Part 4A Permissions (SUP 6) 

Firms can apply to the PRA and/or the FCA to vary a Part 4A permission, including where the firm ceases to carry on any of the regulated activities for which it has Part 4A permission (or expects to do so in the short term, normally less than six months). 

Under Section 20(1) of FSMA – Authorised persons acting without permission, a firm is prohibited from carrying on a regulated activity in the UK (or purporting to do so) otherwise than in accordance with its permission. 

Firms wishing to change their Part 4A permission can apply to the PRA and/or the FCA under Section 44 of FSMA – Variation at request of the authorised person. 

Firms intending to expand their business should assess, taking appropriate professional advice where necessary, whether they will need to apply to the PRA and/or the FCA to vary their Part 4A permission before making any changes. When a firm intends to transfer its business to a different legal entity (for example, the business is to be transferred from a sole trader to a partnership), it will need to apply to the PRA and/or the FCA for cancellation of its Part 4A permission, and the entity to which the business is to be transferred will need to apply for a new Part 4A permission. 

A firm which is seeking to vary its Part 4A permission substantially or cancel its Part 4A permission should discuss its plans with its usual supervisory contact at the PRA and/or the FCA as early as possible before making its application, in order to comply with Principle 11 of the Principles for Businesses. This is to help the PRA and/or the FCA and the firm to agree the correct approach for the firm. When a firm intends to cease carrying on one or more regulated activities permanently, it should give prompt notice to the PRA and/or the FCA to comply with Principle 11. A firm should consider whether it needs to notify the PRA and/or the FCA before applying to vary or cancel its Part 4A permission. 

266 

PRA Rulebook and FCA Handbook of Rules and Guidance 

Under Section 55H or 55L of FSMA (as enacted by the Financial Services Act 2012), a firm may apply to the PRA and/or the FCA to vary its Part 4A permission to: 

1. allow it to carry on further regulated activities 

2. reduce the number of regulated activities it is permitted to carry out 

3. vary the PRA and/or the FCA’s description of its regulated activities (including by the removal or variation of any limitations), or 

4. cancel any requirement applied for by the firm or imposed by the PRA under Section 55M and/or by the FCA under Section 55L of FSMA – imposition of requirements, or 

5. vary any such requirement. 

A firm may apply to vary its Part 4A permission to add a new regulated activity and at the same time remove a regulated activity for which it currently has permission. 

When a firm wishes to cease carrying on an activity for which it has Part 4A permission, it will usually apply to vary its Part 4A permission to remove that activity. If a firm wishes to cease carrying on an activity in relation to any specified investment, it will apply to vary its Part 4A permission to remove that specified investment from the relevant activity. 

When a firm is requesting to amend its Part 4A permission for regulated activities that would cause a significant change in the firm’s business or risk profile, then the regulator may require the firm to complete appropriate parts of the full application pack (see the FCA and/or the PRA website: How do I get authorised) as directed by the PRA regulators FCA. 

Applications for variation involving significant changes may be processed by the firm’s usual supervisory contact at the FCA and/or the PRA, in conjunction with the Permissions department. Examples of an application for variation of Part 4A permission which may represent a significant change include, but are not limited to, an application: 

- to carry on new regulated activities such as accepting deposits 

- to extend the insurance business of a firm which already has Part 4A permission, which includes carrying out or effecting contracts of insurance (or both), to new classes of specified investment, or 

- to remove a requirement preventing a firm from holding or controlling client money, or 

- which causes the firm to change prudential category by, for example, removing a requirement relating to prudential category. 

## 16.1.1 When will the PRA/FCA Grant an Application for Variation of Permission? 

The regulators are required by Section 41(2) of FSMA to ensure that a firm applying to vary its Part 4A permission satisfies and will continue to satisfy the threshold conditions in relation to all the regulated activities for which the firm has or will have Part 4A permission after the variation. 

However, the regulators must take such steps as they consider necessary in relation to a particular firm, to meet any of their regulatory objectives. This may include granting a firm’s application for variation of Part 4A permission when it wishes to wind down (run off) its business activities and cease to carry out new business as a result of no longer being able to satisfy the threshold conditions. 

267 

In addition, they may also refuse the application, if it appears that any of the regulators regulatory objectives would be adversely affected if the application were to be granted, and it is desirable in order to meet their regulatory objectives for the application to be refused. 

## 16.1.2 Commencing New Regulated Activities 

If the variation of the Part 4A permission is given, then the regulators will expect the firm to commence that regulated activity in accordance with its business plan (revised as necessary to take account of changes during the application process), or scheme of operations for an insurer. 

Under Section 55L (FCA) and Section 55M (PRA) of FSMA, the PRA and/or the FCA can refuse an application from a firm to cancel its Part 4A permission if it appears that it is desirable for the application to be refused in order to meet any of the PRA and/or the FCA regulatory objectives. 

A firm will be expected to demonstrate to the regulators that it has ceased carrying on regulated activities. As part of the application process, the regulator may require the firm to provide confirmation or verification from a professional adviser on certain matters to supplement the report, verifying that the activities have been ceased by the firm. 

## 16.2 Waiver and Modification Rules (SUP 8)
